name: SiliconFlash OmniCoder DFlash bootstrap

description: Bootstrap baseline: isolated local oMLX server + OmniCoder-9B bf16 benchmark harness with DFlash scaffolding and autoresearch scripts.
Tune autoresearch harness for the Day-1 single-request target by setting max-concurrent-requests=1, reducing scheduler overhead during the OmniCoder baseline benchmark.
Reduce per-token collector overhead for non-streaming batched LLM requests by raising BatchedEngine's default stream_interval from 1 to 8.
Test a more aggressive stream_interval=16 to see if batching collector updates further improves non-streaming throughput; it regressed request performance versus the 8-token setting.
Probe whether stream_interval=4 gives a better balance than 8 for non-streaming OmniCoder requests; it significantly regressed throughput.
Try a non-aggregating output collector for non-streaming generate() requests so only the latest cumulative output is retained; it did not beat the stream_interval=8 baseline.
Re-run the current stream_interval=8 baseline unchanged to estimate noise; result was below the best sample, so keep existing code but treat future small wins cautiously.
Test a smaller initial_cache_blocks=64 in the autoresearch harness to reduce short-context cache setup overhead; improved over stock but did not beat the current best stream_interval=8 baseline.
Test yielding to the event loop less often in EngineCore when no streamed consumers are waiting, aiming to cut scheduler-loop overhead for non-streaming requests; it regressed throughput.

name: SiliconFlash OmniCoder DFlash warmed-request baseline

description: Rebaseline the session around a warmed-model request: add a tiny distinct warmup request to exclude lazy OmniCoder model load from the primary throughput metric and better expose generation-path improvements.
Confirm the warmed-model baseline under the corrected workload; the unchanged stream_interval=8 configuration produced a slightly faster steady-state sample, so promote it to the current baseline.
Retest stream_interval=16 under the warmed-model workload; the initial +0.9% sample looked promising but was too small relative to expected noise, so hold judgment pending confirmation.
Confirmation run for stream_interval=16 under the warmed-model workload came in below baseline, indicating the earlier uptick was noise rather than a robust win.
Test mutating RequestOutput in place during collector merges to reduce allocation churn in the hot path; it did not beat the current warmed baseline.

name: SiliconFlash OmniCoder DFlash warmed-request 256-token baseline

description: Rebaseline the session around a warmed-model 256-token generation workload so DFlash-style generation-path wins are easier to detect than on the noisier 64-token benchmark.
Confirm the warmed-model 256-token baseline under the corrected workload; the unchanged stream_interval=8 configuration produced a marginally better repeat sample.

Avoid copying the cumulative output_token_ids list on every scheduler step by passing the request-owned list through RequestOutput; result was effectively flat-to-worse on the warmed 256-token workload.
Add a non-streaming request fast path: mark generate() requests as non-streaming and skip per-token detokenization/new_text construction in the scheduler, decoding only once at finalization.
Confirmation run #1 for the kept non-streaming detokenization-skip fast path; throughput remained above the warmed 256-token baseline.
Confirmation run #2 for the kept non-streaming detokenization-skip fast path; throughput again remained above the warmed 256-token baseline and strengthened confidence in the change.

Extend the non-streaming fast path so scheduler outputs are only constructed/emitted at request finish for generate() calls, eliminating intermediate RequestOutput churn on the warmed 256-token workload.
Confirmation run #1 for the kept non-streaming finish-only output fast path; throughput stayed above the prior decode-skip-only baseline.
Confirmation run #2 for the kept non-streaming finish-only output fast path; throughput again remained above the prior decode-skip-only baseline.
Test whether the newer non-streaming finish-only output path makes the earlier stream_interval=8 tuning unnecessary by reverting BatchedEngine back to stream_interval=1; it regressed throughput.

Test skipping RequestStreamState allocation for non-streaming requests after adding the finish-only output path; this broke response delivery with premature chunked termination, indicating another path still assumes stream state exists.

Safer follow-up to the stream-state crash: keep RequestStreamState intact but disable RequestOutputCollector aggregation for non-streaming requests now that they only emit once on finish; it did not beat the current best.

Try bypassing RequestOutputCollector entirely for non-streaming generate() calls by storing the finished RequestOutput directly in EngineCore state and waking generate() via the existing event; the first run looked strong but failed to reproduce on confirmation, so treat it as noise.
On top of the kept finish-only non-streaming path, avoid copying the cumulative output_token_ids list when building the final RequestOutput; it did not improve throughput.
For non-streaming finish-only outputs, avoid allocating the final new_token_ids list because generate() never consumes incremental token IDs; the initial gain did not reproduce on confirmation.

Assume the finish-only non-streaming path leaves exactly one final collector entry and replace the drain loop in generate() with a single get_nowait(); this badly regressed throughput, so the collector can still accumulate multiple outputs in practice.
Retest stream_interval=16 on top of the kept non-streaming finish-only fast path, but the run was aborted before producing a valid measurement.
Test an idle-engine direct synchronous generation fast path that bypasses the background async loop for plain non-streaming text requests; it regressed the warmed 256-token workload.
Make the benchmark explicitly greedy by setting top_p=1.0 alongside temperature=0 in both warmup and measured requests; it did not improve throughput over the current baseline.
Revisit reduced event-loop yields under the newer non-streaming finish-only fast path: yield immediately on finishes, otherwise only when a streamed consumer is waiting or every few steps.
Confirmation run #1 for the kept reduced-yield engine-loop change on the non-streaming finish-only path; throughput remained above the prior baseline.
Confirmation run #2 for the kept reduced-yield engine-loop change on the non-streaming finish-only path; throughput again remained above the prior baseline.
Probe a more aggressive reduced-yield cadence by yielding every 16 scheduler steps instead of 8 on the non-streaming finish-only path; it did not beat the current best.
Bracket the reduced-yield cadence on the other side by yielding every 4 scheduler steps instead of 8 on the non-streaming finish-only path; it did not beat the current best.

Diagnostic upper-bound run with --no-cache to estimate how much paged-SSD cache work contributes to latency on the warmed 256-token workload; it did not beat the current best and is not acceptable as a kept direction because SiliconFlash must preserve paged SSD caching.

Test the most aggressive reduced-yield strategy on the non-streaming finish-only path by yielding only on finished outputs or waiting streamed consumers, with no periodic yield at all; it regressed back to baseline.

Use a per-request asyncio.Future for non-streaming generate() completion and have the engine loop fulfill it directly on finished output instead of draining a collector; it regressed throughput versus the kept path.
Re-run the unchanged current best-known code to refresh the baseline sample; this run was a severe slow outlier and should be treated as noise, not a code regression.

name: SiliconFlash OmniCoder DFlash warmed-request 256-token median-of-3 baseline

description: Rebaseline around a median-of-3 measured workload to reduce noise, but repeated benchmark requests inside one server run became progressively slower (about 30s, 36s, 47s) and made the workload pathological instead of cleaner.

name: SiliconFlash OmniCoder DFlash warmed-request 256-token baseline

description: Restore the warmed-model single-request 256-token workload after discarding the pathological median-of-3 design; use this as the new session baseline and re-measure immediately to separate benchmark reset effects from real regressions.

Immediate rerun of the unchanged current code on the restored warmed single-request workload confirmed that throughput is still in the ~10 tok/s range, so the new low baseline is currently real rather than a one-off spike.
Reset the per-run paged-SSD cache directory in autoresearch.sh to eliminate stale cache-state pollution between experiments; this restores a much healthier warmed-request benchmark range.
Confirmation run #1 for the per-run paged-SSD cache reset benchmark fix; throughput climbed further into the high-teens, confirming the prior low baseline was polluted by stale cache state.
Confirmation run #2 for the per-run paged-SSD cache reset benchmark fix; throughput remained well above the polluted baseline, stabilizing the restored workload in the high-teens.

Rerun the unchanged current best-known code after fixing per-run cache pollution; the restored workload is now consistently landing in the mid-to-high teens, not back at the polluted ~10 tok/s baseline.

Align the benchmark with the intended greedy DFlash-style workload by setting chat_template_kwargs.enable_thinking=false for both warmup and measured requests; this restores throughput to the high-20 tok/s range.
Confirmation run #1 for the kept no-thinking benchmark configuration; throughput remained essentially unchanged in the high-20 tok/s range.
Confirmation run #2 for the kept no-thinking benchmark configuration; throughput dipped slightly but remained far above the polluted thinking-enabled baseline.
Retest smaller initial_cache_blocks on the cleaned no-thinking workload by lowering it from 64 to 16; it did not improve throughput.
Retest stream_interval=16 on top of the cleaned no-thinking workload and current non-streaming fast path; it remained worse than the existing setting.

Retest stream_interval=4 on the cleaned no-thinking workload; the first two runs were competitive, but a third confirmation run collapsed into a severe slow outlier, so it is not robust enough to replace the current setting.
Re-test explicit top_p=1.0 on top of the cleaned no-thinking benchmark; it caused a dramatic regression, so the server’s default greedy path is clearly preferable here.
Skip clean_special_tokens() in BatchedEngine.generate for the no-thinking non-streaming path; it regressed throughput substantially, so the cleanup step is not the bottleneck.
Remove accidentally retained top_p=1.0 from the warmup request in autoresearch.sh and re-anchor the OmniCoder-9B bf16 benchmark on the intended no-thinking deterministic workload.

asi.hypothesis: A leftover warmup top_p override from a discarded probe is polluting the bf16 benchmark setup; removing it should restore the intended warmed-model baseline.
asi.learning: The warmup override was indeed stray harness state. Removing it restored throughput to ~28.2 tok/s, consistent with the prior bf16 range.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue bf16-only optimization in scheduler/engine hot paths; avoid quantized or alternate-target detours.

description: Try a dedicated final-output handoff for non-streaming generate() requests to bypass collector bookkeeping; it was slightly slower than the current OmniCoder-9B bf16 baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Skipping collector/state bookkeeping for non-streaming generate() requests may reduce Python overhead on the warmed 256-token bf16 benchmark.
asi.rollback_reason: Measured throughput dropped from the re-anchored ~28.2 tok/s baseline to 27.5 tok/s, so the extra direct-handoff plumbing did not help.
asi.next_action_hint: Focus next on scheduler finish-path work such as output object construction and cache-store/finalization overhead rather than engine-core handoff changes.
asi.learning: Collector bypass for non-streaming requests is not a reliable win in the current architecture; the remaining cost is likely deeper in scheduler finalization or cache handling.

description: Diagnostic upper-bound run with paged-SSD cache disabled in the harness to test whether _cleanup_finished cache storage dominates the measured request; it did not materially improve throughput.

asi.hypothesis: If paged-SSD cache storage/finalization is on the critical path, removing it should noticeably raise OmniCoder-9B bf16 request throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: Throughput stayed below the current ~28.2 tok/s baseline, so cache-store work is not the dominant bottleneck for this workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Restore full cache semantics and continue targeting scheduler response construction or MLX generation-path overhead instead of cache-store deferral.
asi.learning: _cleanup_finished runs before the scheduler step returns, but disabling paged-SSD cache did not yield a win here; the main remaining cost lies elsewhere.

description: Try reusing request.output_token_ids directly in the scheduler’s final RequestOutput instead of copying the list; the first run looked neutral-to-slightly positive, but the confirmation run fell below baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Avoiding the final output_token_ids list copy may shave a small amount of Python overhead on the OmniCoder-9B bf16 non-streaming finish path.
asi.rollback_reason: The confirmation run dropped to 27.5 tok/s, so any apparent gain was within noise and not worth keeping.
asi.next_action_hint: Target larger remaining hot spots than one final list copy, likely around response construction or generation-step overhead.
asi.learning: The final token-list copy is not a meaningful bottleneck on this workload.

description: First end-to-end DFlash benchmark for omni9b-phase2prime using the z-lab drafter through the new MLX target bridge; it executed successfully but was far slower than stock generation.

asi.hypothesis: With the z-lab DFlash loop bridged into oMLX, omni9b-phase2prime should begin showing speculative-decoding gains or at least establish a runnable baseline.
asi.rollback_reason: The bridged prototype only reached 17.7 tok/s versus the ~28.2 tok/s stock bf16 baseline, so the current bridge/interop overhead dominates any speculative gain.
asi.next_action_hint: Profile where time is being spent in the bridge path—especially MLX<->torch conversions and repeated target hidden-state materialization—and reduce or eliminate those crossings.
asi.learning: The DFlash path is now genuinely runnable through oMLX for omni9b-phase2prime, but the current torch-bridge prototype is too expensive to keep as a serving path.

description: Rebuild and optimize the runnable DFlash path for omni9b-phase2prime: restore the z-lab drafter + MLX target bridge, capture only required conditioning layers, and switch bf16 MLX->torch transfers onto a fast fp16 DLPack path. This recovers the server benchmark from 17.7 to 26.5 tok/s and keeps a working native-integration baseline for further DFlash optimization.

asi.hypothesis: Most of the bridged DFlash slowdown comes from excessive MLX<->torch conversion overhead, especially converting every hidden state and falling back to numpy for bf16 tensors.
asi.learning: Selective hidden-state capture plus bf16->fp16 DLPack transfers materially reduced bridge overhead and restored the runnable DFlash server path to near-baseline throughput. The remaining gap is still dominated by MLX->torch crossings, but the path is now viable enough to iterate on in-tree.
asi.next_action_hint: Target the remaining _mx_to_torch hot spots by reducing conversion frequency further—especially logits/hidden-state transfers per target pass—or by moving more of the acceptance loop fully into MLX.
asi.profiling: Direct 32-token DFlash probe improved from ~6.48 tok/s to ~10.04 tok/s after the transfer changes; full server benchmark improved to 26.48 tok/s.

description: Reduce greedy DFlash bridge overhead further by having the MLX target bridge return sampled token IDs directly, so the z-lab loop no longer converts full target logits just to run argmax on the target side.

asi.hypothesis: On the current greedy-only omni9b-phase2prime DFlash path, crossing full target logits into torch is wasted work; returning only sampled token IDs should shave bridge overhead.
asi.learning: The direct DFlash probe improved slightly again and the full server benchmark moved from 26.48 to 26.69 tok/s, confirming that target-logit transfer was a real but smaller remaining cost than hidden-state transfer.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep attacking _mx_to_torch by reducing hidden-state materialization/casts further or moving the acceptance comparison loop into MLX.
asi.profiling: Direct 32-token DFlash probe improved from ~10.04 tok/s to ~10.25 tok/s after replacing target-logit transfer with sampled-token transfer.

description: Fuse selected conditioning layers on the MLX side before crossing to torch, to reduce DFlash bridge call count; the direct probe looked roughly flat and the full server benchmark regressed slightly versus the current kept DFlash baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Combining the selected target hidden states into one fused tensor before MLX->torch transfer may reduce bridge overhead enough to improve end-to-end DFlash throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: Despite halving _mx_to_torch call count, the full server benchmark dropped back from 26.69 to 26.48 tok/s, so the extra fusion/cast work did not pay off.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the sampled-token shortcut, revert fused-layer transfer, and target other bridge costs such as torch->MLX crossings or repeated cache/embedding conversions.
asi.learning: Reducing bridge call count alone is not sufficient if the fused transfer itself becomes more expensive; the best kept DFlash path remains the sampled-token shortcut without pre-fusing hidden states.

description: Make the DFlash loop device-aware so target outputs can be moved onto the drafter device cleanly; this enabled an MPS probe, but the normal CPU-backed server path was slightly slower than the current kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Generalizing the DFlash loop to handle non-CPU drafter devices cleanly may unlock faster Apple Silicon backends without hurting the current CPU path.
asi.rollback_reason: The CPU-backed server benchmark slipped from the kept 26.69 tok/s baseline to 26.58 tok/s, and the direct MPS probe was much slower (~5 tok/s), so this refactor is not worth keeping yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Stay on the CPU-backed torch drafter path and continue optimizing bridge conversions inside the kept baseline rather than broadening device support.
asi.learning: Torch MPS is not a win for this bridged DFlash path right now, and even making the loop device-flexible adds enough overhead to lose a bit on the current CPU baseline.

description: Override the z-lab drafter to use deeper OmniCoder-biased conditioning layers by default; although a small acceptance sweep looked better, end-to-end DFlash throughput collapsed badly.

asi.hypothesis: OmniCoder-9B may prefer deeper target hidden-state layers than the stock Qwen3.5 DFlash layer set, and improving acceptance should raise speculative throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark collapsed to 10.3 tok/s, so the deeper conditioning choice severely hurt runtime despite looking better on a narrow acceptance-only probe.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not optimize on acceptance counts alone; profile any future conditioning-layer changes against end-to-end throughput immediately.
asi.learning: For the current bridged DFlash implementation, the stock Qwen3.5 conditioning layout performs much better end-to-end than the deeper OmniCoder-biased layer sets tested.

description: Allow the runtime to override the z-lab drafter block size from config and test block_size=8 instead of 16 for omni9b-phase2prime; it looked better in a tiny direct probe but not on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Because acceptance is low on the current bridged DFlash path, a smaller speculative block (8 instead of 16) may reduce wasted draft work and improve end-to-end throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark slipped to 26.39 tok/s, so the smaller block did not beat the kept block_size=16 baseline.
asi.next_action_hint: Treat tiny direct probes as directional only; continue validating all promising DFlash changes against the full warmed 256-token server benchmark immediately.
asi.learning: On this workload, the current server path still prefers the original 16-token DFlash block despite a smaller block looking better in short direct probes.

description: Add a hybrid DFlash path that replaces the torch z-lab drafter forward pass with the new MLX-native 5-layer drafter port while keeping the current MLX target bridge. This raises the full omni9b-phase2prime server benchmark from 26.69 to 26.98 tok/s and proves the native drafter port is viable in the real runtime.

asi.hypothesis: Replacing the torch drafter forward pass with an MLX-native checkpoint-compatible drafter should reduce the remaining bridge overhead enough to beat the current kept DFlash baseline.
asi.learning: The native MLX drafter is now checkpoint-loadable, executable, and beneficial in the full server path. Even as a hybrid with the existing target bridge, it outperformed the torch-based drafter path and produced a new best DFlash result.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep replacing the remaining torch-side pieces around the hybrid path—especially draft-loop token bookkeeping and cache semantics—to convert this from a hybrid runtime into a fully MLX-native DFlash loop.
asi.profiling: Direct hybrid-native probe improved substantially after the native RoPE fix, and the full server benchmark reached 26.98 tok/s.

description: Keep hybrid-native DFlash conditioning features and embeddings on MLX to halve bridge transfer count, but the full server benchmark regressed despite the reduced crossing count.

asi.hypothesis: Removing the MLX->torch->MLX round-trip for native hybrid conditioning features and embeddings should lower bridge overhead enough to improve end-to-end DFlash throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark dropped from the kept 26.98 tok/s hybrid-native baseline to 26.31 tok/s, so the changed dataflow did not pay off.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the earlier hybrid-native path and look for correctness/performance wins in native draft-cache semantics or acceptance behavior rather than this particular hidden-state handoff shape.
asi.learning: Halving bridge transfer count is not sufficient by itself; the resulting fused/native handoff shape can still cost more in the real server path than the simpler previous hybrid-native baseline.

description: Port the native drafter toward the public eauchs/mlx-dflash structure with packed QKV, packed gate/up, explicit draft KV cache objects, and bf16 weight loading. The full omni9b-phase2prime server benchmark stayed essentially flat/slightly worse versus the kept precision-fix hybrid-native path.

asi.hypothesis: Adopting the public MLX-native drafter structure from eauchs/mlx-dflash should improve speculative quality or dispatch efficiency enough to beat the current hybrid-native baseline.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark came in slightly below the kept 28.13 tok/s baseline, and the short direct probe showed worse local generation behavior, so this exact port shape is not a net win yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler precision-fixed native drafter path, then port only the reference implementation pieces that are clearly isolated wins in our stack—especially single-sync decode structure and any target-side hidden-state / head_dim=256 fast-path patches.
asi.learning: The public reference architecture does not transfer 1:1 into the current oMLX hybrid runtime. Precision alignment was a real win, but the packed/bf16 drafter rewrite alone did not improve end-to-end throughput here and may require accompanying target/runtime changes to pay off.

description: Restore the native-drafter precision fix and additionally remove the draft embedding proxy round-trip by using target.embed_tokens_mx() directly in the hybrid-native loop. The full server benchmark stayed below the earlier precision-fix-only result, so the embedding handoff change is not a net win in this stack.

asi.hypothesis: Keeping native-drafter inputs at the draft weight dtype and removing one torch<->MLX embedding round-trip should preserve the acceptance gain while shaving sync/transfer cost.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark reached 28.00 tok/s, below the earlier 28.13 precision-fix result, so the direct embed_tokens_mx handoff did not improve end-to-end throughput.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep only the precision-alignment fix and benchmark it cleanly as its own experiment; continue using the public reference repo mainly for target-side hidden-state and single-sync decode ideas rather than blindly removing every bridge crossing.
asi.learning: In this hybrid runtime, eliminating the draft embedding proxy round-trip is not automatically beneficial. The useful signal was precision alignment into the native drafter, not this particular embedding-path rewrite.

description: Align hybrid-native drafter inputs with the native drafter weight dtype instead of forcing fp16 downcasts. Across repeated runs this lifts the MLX-native hybrid DFlash path from the prior kept 26.98 tok/s baseline into an approximately 28.0 tok/s band on omni9b-phase2prime, effectively recovering stock-level throughput while preserving the native drafter integration path.

asi.hypothesis: The native MLX drafter was loaded in float32, so forcing target_hidden and noise embeddings to fp16 before the draft forward was hurting speculative quality and acceptance; matching the draft input dtype to the model weights should improve end-to-end throughput.
asi.learning: Precision alignment into the native drafter is a real win. The effect is modest but repeatable: the hybrid-native DFlash path now runs around 28.0 tok/s instead of 26.98 tok/s, while extra embedding-path handoff changes were not beneficial.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep this precision-aligned hybrid-native path and focus next on target-side ideas from the public MLX reference: head_dim=256 fast-path verification, hidden-state extraction/patching strategy, and single-sync decode structure.
asi.reruns: Observed 28.09, 27.84, and 28.04 tok/s on confirmation runs after the fix; treat this as a stable ~28.0 tok/s band rather than a single-sample spike.

description: Override native-hybrid DFlash conditioning layers to an OmniCoder-tuned evenly spaced set (1,7,13,19,25). Although the direct native probe looked much better, the full omni9b-phase2prime server benchmark collapsed badly.

asi.hypothesis: Retargeting the native drafter conditioning to OmniCoder-local hidden-state layers should improve acceptance enough to unlock the native path on omni9b-phase2prime.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark dropped from the kept ~28.0 tok/s DFlash baseline to 15.71 tok/s, so this layer override is not viable despite promising microprobe acceptance.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not trust native-path acceptance microprobes alone. Keep the official layer set in the shipped path and continue structural debugging around target-side native integration or reference-target validation.
asi.learning: A layer set that improves the narrow native probe can still catastrophically hurt the real server workload on omni9b-phase2prime.

description: Switch the native drafter from MLX KVCache trim semantics to an exact-crop draft KV cache so the native target-side loop matches the hybrid path's token/state evolution step-for-step. This fixed a real correctness divergence in direct probes but did not improve the full omni9b-phase2prime server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The native drafter was leaking rejected draft tokens because KVCache trim semantics did not match DFlash's exact draft-cache crop behavior; replacing it with an exact-crop draft cache should improve end-to-end throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: Despite fixing the native/hybrid divergence in direct token traces, the full server benchmark remained flat at 28.04 tok/s versus the kept baseline, so it is not a primary-metric win by itself.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep using the exact-crop insight for future native-target integration work, but focus the next experiment on reducing runtime overhead in the native target loop or validating against a standard Qwen3.5-9B bf16 reference target outside the primary path.
asi.learning: Exact draft-cache crop semantics were a real correctness bug in the native target-side probe, but they are not the main throughput limiter in the current server path.

description: Cache the repeated draft mask-token embeddings in the hybrid-native DFlash loop and only embed the current last token each step. This removes redundant target embedding work for the 15 masked positions in every draft block and lifts omni9b-phase2prime DFlash throughput into a stable ~28.3 tok/s band.

asi.hypothesis: In the hybrid-native DFlash loop, the draft input is always [last_token] + [mask×15], so re-embedding all 16 tokens every step wastes work. Reusing a precomputed mask embedding block should cut per-step target embedding overhead and improve throughput.
asi.learning: The repeated masked positions are worth caching. Across repeated full server runs the change improved the DFlash path from the prior ~28.0 tok/s baseline to roughly 28.28–28.36 tok/s.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue trimming repeated per-step work in the hybrid-native loop, especially other fixed-shape conversions around the draft input and target-hidden handoff, but keep validating with the full 256-token server benchmark rather than microprobes alone.
asi.reruns: Observed 28.33, 28.36, and 28.28 tok/s on repeated full benchmark runs after this change.

description: Switch the cached draft-input embedding path to use direct MLX token embeddings inside the target bridge instead of the existing torch-facing embed proxy. This regressed the full server benchmark versus the new cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: After caching the repeated mask embeddings, using direct MLX token embeddings for the remaining last-token embed should remove another torch->MLX conversion and improve throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark dropped from the kept ~28.3 tok/s cached-mask baseline to 28.01 tok/s, so this narrower embedding-path rewrite is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the cached-mask optimization but leave the remaining last-token embedding on the prior bridge path; continue targeting other repeated per-step work instead of this embedding handoff.
asi.learning: The per-step draft-input embedding cost is only partly worth attacking. Caching the repeated mask embeddings helped, but replacing the remaining last-token embed proxy with the direct MLX path did not improve end-to-end throughput.

description: Build each verify block as torch.cat([last_token, predicted_ids]) instead of cloning and mutating the masked output slice in the hybrid-native DFlash loop. This regressed versus the cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Avoiding the per-step clone and mutation of the 16-token masked block should shave Python/tensor overhead in the hybrid-native DFlash loop.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark fell below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this block-construction rewrite is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the cached-mask optimization and continue focusing on genuinely repeated cross-framework work rather than small tensor assembly changes.
asi.learning: The block clone/mutation is not a dominant cost in the current hybrid-native path; removing it did not improve end-to-end throughput.

description: Fuse the selected target hidden states into one tensor before converting MLX->torch in the hybrid-native path, reducing per-step conversion count. This remained slightly below the cached-mask baseline on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Halving the number of MLX->torch conversions for target hidden states should improve hybrid-native DFlash throughput on top of the cached-mask baseline.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask result, so the fused hidden-state handoff is not a net win in this runtime shape.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the cached-mask optimization and continue looking for repeated per-step work that can be removed without perturbing the hidden-state handoff layout.
asi.learning: The dominant remaining bridge cost is not solved simply by fusing the selected hidden states; that handoff shape still performs slightly worse end-to-end here.

description: Reuse a preallocated verify-block tensor in the hybrid-native DFlash loop instead of cloning a fresh block slice each step. This regressed the full server benchmark relative to the cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Reusing a preallocated verify block should remove small per-step tensor allocation and mutation costs in the hybrid-native DFlash loop.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark dropped below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this buffer reuse does not help end-to-end throughput.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler cached-mask implementation and continue focusing on costs with proven impact rather than small tensor assembly optimizations.
asi.learning: Per-step verify-block tensor allocation is not a dominant bottleneck in the current hybrid-native path.

description: Limit the hybrid-native stop-token check to the newly produced output slice instead of scanning the full generated suffix each step. This regressed the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The per-step stop-token scan over the entire generated suffix is unnecessary work for the benchmark workload; restricting it to newly produced tokens should slightly improve throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark dropped below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this narrower stop-token check is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler existing stop check and keep focusing on costs that have already shown measurable impact in repeated runs.
asi.learning: The stop-token scan is not a meaningful bottleneck on the current warmed 256-token workload.

description: Re-test exact-crop draft KV cache semantics on top of the cached-mask baseline. The full server benchmark still regressed despite the native direct-loop correctness benefits.

asi.hypothesis: Now that repeated mask embeddings are cached, exact draft-cache crop semantics might turn into an end-to-end win in the hybrid-native path as well.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so exact-crop draft cache semantics are still not a primary-metric win in the server path.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep exact-crop semantics as a known correctness requirement for native-target integration, but continue optimizing the current hybrid-native server path separately.
asi.learning: Exact draft-cache crop is important for native-loop parity but does not improve the current server benchmark even after the cached-mask optimization.

description: Keep target posterior token IDs on MLX through acceptance computation and convert only the single bonus token needed for output assembly. This reduced some MLX->torch traffic but still underperformed the cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Avoiding full posterior MLX->torch conversion in the hybrid-native loop should reduce bridge overhead enough to improve throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so the MX-posterior path is not a net win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the cached-mask optimization and continue chasing larger structural costs rather than shaving this particular posterior conversion path.
asi.learning: Posterior-token conversion is not the dominant remaining overhead once the mask-embedding cache is in place.

description: Materialize the cached mask-token embeddings through the existing torch embed proxy first, then convert once to MLX, instead of calling _torch_to_mx on the proxy result directly. Repeated runs landed effectively tied with the kept cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Building the reusable mask-token embedding block through the existing torch embed path and converting once might be marginally cheaper than the current materialization route.
asi.rollback_reason: Across repeated runs this variant was effectively tied with the kept cached-mask baseline, so the tiny difference is not actionable.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler cached-mask baseline and continue looking for larger structural wins rather than sub-noise embedding materialization variants.
asi.learning: The cached mask-embedding idea is the real win; the exact one-time construction route for that cache block is within noise on the full benchmark.
asi.reruns: Observed 28.37, 28.36, and 28.38 tok/s; treat as tied with the kept ~28.3 tok/s baseline.

description: Re-measure the current stock omni9b-phase2prime non-DFlash path against the latest hybrid-native DFlash baseline. Stock remains effectively tied with the kept ~28.3 tok/s DFlash path on the warmed 256-token workload.

asi.hypothesis: The latest cached-mask DFlash baseline may now have pulled clearly ahead of stock on the current workload, so a fresh stock comparator is needed.
asi.rollback_reason: No code change and the stock path measured essentially tied with the kept DFlash baseline, so there is no new optimization to keep.
asi.next_action_hint: Treat the current situation as parity between stock and DFlash on this workload and continue pursuing structural acceptance/runtime improvements rather than assuming DFlash is already ahead in a meaningful way.
asi.learning: On the current warmed 256-token benchmark, stock is still about 28.36 tok/s, essentially matching the latest DFlash result.

description: Load the MLX-native drafter weights as bf16 instead of float32 to reduce MLX-side bandwidth and compute cost. This regressed the full omni9b-phase2prime server benchmark versus the kept cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The hybrid-native path may benefit from lower-precision drafter weights now that the main state/caching bugs are fixed, potentially improving MLX-side throughput without hurting acceptance too much.
asi.rollback_reason: The full server benchmark dropped below the kept cached-mask baseline, so bf16-loading the native drafter is not a win here.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep float32-loaded native drafter weights for the current hybrid path and test fp16 loading separately if continuing to probe precision/bandwidth tradeoffs.
asi.learning: The native drafter is sensitive to precision in this hybrid runtime. Lowering weight precision to bf16 lost more than it gained.

description: Load the MLX-native drafter weights as fp16 instead of float32 to reduce MLX-side cost while staying closer to the previous float32 baseline than bf16. This still regressed the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: fp16 drafter weights might preserve enough acceptance quality while reducing MLX-side overhead better than float32 in the hybrid-native path.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark still fell below the kept cached-mask baseline, so fp16-loading the native drafter is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep float32-loaded native drafter weights and continue targeting bridge/runtime overhead rather than further simple precision downgrades.
asi.learning: Both bf16 and fp16 native drafter loads underperform the float32-loaded hybrid baseline on this workload.

description: Re-test DFlash block_size=8 on the current cached-mask hybrid-native baseline to see whether smaller speculative blocks help when acceptance remains low. It still underperformed the kept block_size=16 baseline on the full server workload.

asi.hypothesis: With acceptance still relatively low, reducing the speculative block size from 16 to 8 might reduce wasted verify work and improve end-to-end throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask block_size=16 baseline, so block_size=8 is still not the right setting for this workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep block_size=16 and continue targeting runtime overhead or acceptance quality instead of smaller speculative blocks.
asi.learning: Even after recent hybrid-native improvements, block_size=8 still loses to 16 on the real omni9b-phase2prime server benchmark.

description: Re-test the hybrid-native DFlash path with a much smaller speculative block (`block_size=4`) to see whether the low-acceptance regime benefits from less wasted verify work. It still underperformed the kept block_size=16 baseline.

asi.hypothesis: If acceptance remains relatively low on omni9b-phase2prime, reducing the speculative block to 4 may cut wasted verify work enough to beat the current block_size=16 baseline.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask block_size=16 baseline, so block_size=4 is not beneficial on this workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep block_size=16 and continue looking for structural acceptance or bridge/runtime wins rather than smaller speculative blocks.
asi.learning: Even aggressively shrinking the speculative block does not help the current hybrid-native server path on omni9b-phase2prime.

description: Return fused conditioning hidden states directly on MLX from the target bridge so the hybrid-native loop can skip the per-step torch.cat plus torch->MLX round-trip for target_hidden. This still regressed versus the cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Eliminating the per-step hidden-state torch handoff should reduce bridge overhead enough to improve the hybrid-native path.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this MLX hidden-state handoff variant is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the cached-mask baseline and continue focusing on larger remaining bridge/runtime costs rather than the target_hidden handoff alone.
asi.learning: Even removing the explicit target_hidden bounce is not sufficient to beat the current cached-mask baseline in the server path.

description: Elide the explicit mx.eval() inside the MLX->torch bridge conversion and rely on the conversion itself as the synchronization point. This did not beat the cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The target bridge may be over-synchronizing by calling mx.eval before every MLX->torch conversion; removing that explicit sync could improve the hybrid-native path.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this sync-elision attempt is not a win in the current bridge implementation.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the current bridge conversion behavior and continue searching for larger wins than this local synchronization tweak.
asi.learning: The explicit mx.eval in _mx_to_torch is not the dominant remaining cost on the full server workload.

description: Cache the single-token draft-input embedding across steps when the same last token repeats, on top of the cached-mask baseline. This regressed the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The hybrid-native path may waste work re-embedding the same last token across repeated low-acceptance steps, so caching the one-token embedding could help.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark fell below the kept cached-mask baseline, so the one-token embedding cache is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep only the repeated mask-embedding cache and avoid further small embedding memoization ideas unless a profile clearly points there.
asi.learning: Re-embedding the current last token is not a dominant enough cost to justify this memoization in the current server path.

description: Add an opt-in full MLX native target-loop DFlash execution path in the runtime, using rollback-capable Qwen3.5 target caches and the corrected native probe semantics. This remained slower than the kept cached-mask hybrid path on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Now that native target rollback and draft crop semantics are understood, wiring the full native target loop into the real runtime may beat the hybrid bridge path on the benchmark workload.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask hybrid baseline, so this first in-runtime native target-loop integration is not yet competitive.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the hybrid-native cached-mask path as the main baseline and continue using the native target loop as a structural debugging scaffold rather than the shipping path.
asi.learning: The corrected native target-loop semantics are runnable in the real runtime now, but they still do not outperform the bridge-based hybrid path on this workload.

description: Re-test a larger scheduler stream interval (`16`) on top of the current hybrid-native DFlash baseline. It still regressed on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: DFlash may prefer a more aggressive non-streaming scheduler cadence than the current global stream_interval=8, reducing coordinator overhead on the one-request benchmark.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark dropped below the kept cached-mask baseline, so stream_interval=16 is still not beneficial for this workload even with DFlash enabled.
asi.next_action_hint: Restore stream_interval=8 and, if continuing scheduler experiments, test only narrower DFlash-specific cadence changes rather than global 16-token batching.
asi.learning: The current DFlash path still prefers the existing stream_interval=8 setting over 16 on the real server benchmark.

description: Re-test a smaller scheduler stream interval (`4`) on top of the current hybrid-native DFlash baseline. It also regressed on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: If DFlash steps create different coordinator behavior than stock generation, a smaller stream interval may reduce internal waiting and improve throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so stream_interval=4 is also not beneficial for this workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Restore stream_interval=8 and stop spending time on global scheduler cadence variants for the current DFlash benchmark.
asi.learning: The current DFlash path still prefers stream_interval=8 over both 4 and 16 on the warmed 256-token server benchmark.

description: Construct the DFlash runtime lazily on first DFlash request instead of during engine startup. This reduced startup-path eagerness but regressed the warmed benchmark request throughput.

asi.hypothesis: Moving DFlash runtime construction off startup and onto the first DFlash request may reduce startup overhead without hurting the warmed benchmark request, or at least reveal a better placement of that cost.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark request slowed materially, so lazy DFlash construction is not a win for the current warmed workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep eager DFlash runtime construction and continue targeting request-path costs directly.
asi.learning: Deferring DFlash runtime construction pushes enough work into the request/warmup path to hurt the actual benchmark.

description: Cache the torch mask-token ID tensor across requests in the hybrid-native DFlash loop so it is not recreated per request. This did not beat the kept cached-mask baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The mask-token ID tensor is constant for a given block size and device, so caching it across requests may shave a small amount of request setup overhead in the hybrid-native path.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this cross-request tensor cache is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler per-request mask-token ID construction and continue focusing on larger repeated costs than this tiny setup allocation.
asi.learning: Cross-request caching of the mask-token ID tensor is too small to matter on the current workload.

description: Persist the cached mask embedding block on the drafter model so it survives across requests instead of being rebuilt per request. This did not improve the full benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The reusable mask embedding block may still be costing enough per request that persisting it across requests could improve the warmed benchmark.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so cross-request mask-embedding persistence is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep only the within-request mask-embedding cache and continue targeting bigger structural costs.
asi.learning: The main gain is caching the repeated mask positions within a request; persisting that cache across requests does not materially help on this workload.

description: Wrap the DFlash runtime generate path in torch.inference_mode() to reduce bridge-side torch overhead. This regressed the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The hybrid-native path still does enough torch-side work that forcing inference_mode around the whole DFlash generate path might reduce autograd-related overhead and improve throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark dropped below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this torch.inference_mode wrap is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler runtime generate path and continue targeting overheads that have shown real benchmark sensitivity.
asi.learning: Autograd disabling at this outer runtime scope does not improve the current DFlash bridge path on the real workload.

description: Delay construction of the torch input_ids tensor inside DFlashRuntime.generate() until right before the selected execution path needs it. This regressed the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: A tiny amount of request-path work may be removable by delaying torch tensor construction until after DFlash request setup is complete.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark dropped below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this request-path rearrangement is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler input_ids construction path and continue focusing on larger measured costs.
asi.learning: Reordering torch input tensor construction does not help the current DFlash request path on the warmed workload.

description: Cache prompt tokenization inside the DFlash runtime so repeated deterministic requests can skip tokenizer.encode(prompt). This did not improve the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The warmed benchmark repeatedly uses the same deterministic prompt, so caching its tokenization inside the DFlash runtime may shave a bit of request-path overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask baseline, so prompt-token caching is not a win for the current workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep prompt handling simple and continue focusing on more material runtime costs than tokenization setup.
asi.learning: Prompt tokenization is not a meaningful bottleneck in the current warmed DFlash benchmark path.

description: Try a direct bf16 DLPack MLX->torch bridge path by removing the current bf16->fp16 cast before conversion. This did not improve the full benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: If torch in this environment can consume MLX bf16 arrays directly through DLPack efficiently, removing the cast to fp16 could reduce bridge overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so the direct bf16 DLPack conversion path is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the existing bf16->fp16 bridge conversion and continue targeting larger remaining costs.
asi.learning: The bf16->fp16 cast is not the main remaining bridge bottleneck on the real server workload.

description: Port only the packed-QKV attention layout into the native MLX drafter while keeping the current float32 loading path, to isolate dispatch reduction from the earlier full public-style rewrite. This still regressed the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The public packed-QKV draft attention may reduce MLX dispatch overhead enough to improve the hybrid-native path even without adopting the full public bf16 rewrite.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark fell below the kept cached-mask baseline, so packed-QKV alone is not a net win in this current runtime shape.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the simpler native drafter attention path for now and continue prioritizing empirically winning request-path optimizations over isolated architecture cleanups.
asi.learning: The packed-QKV draft attention layout does not transfer as a standalone performance win into the current hybrid-native server path.

description: Change the harness so the warmup request runs with dflash=false and only the measured benchmark request uses dflash=true, isolating DFlash to the primary request. This regressed the actual benchmark request throughput.

asi.hypothesis: Warming the target model without exercising the DFlash path might leave a cleaner benchmark request and improve measured DFlash throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The benchmark request got materially slower, so this warmup isolation does not help the primary workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep warmup and benchmark on the same effective serving path and continue optimizing the request path itself rather than trying to separate warmup behavior.
asi.learning: For the current workload, exercising the same DFlash path during warmup appears beneficial rather than harmful.

description: Cache the loaded DFlash drafter model process-wide in BatchedEngine startup so repeated server startups in the same Python process can reuse it. This regressed the full benchmark request throughput.

asi.hypothesis: The autoresearch workload repeatedly starts new servers in the same process tree, so process-wide drafter reuse may reduce startup/path setup overhead enough to help the warmed benchmark request.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark request slowed materially, so this process-wide drafter cache is not a win for the current workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep per-start drafter initialization as-is and continue targeting request-path costs instead of cross-start caches.
asi.learning: Cross-start/process-local drafter caching does not translate into better benchmark request throughput in the current harness.

description: Batch the MLX->torch bridge outputs into a single pre-cast + single mx.eval() step in the target bridge, then convert them to torch without per-array syncs. This regressed the full benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The hybrid-native path may still be paying too many decode-cycle syncs in the MLX target bridge; evaluating sampled tokens and selected hidden states together could cut bridge overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The primary benchmark slowed materially, so collapsing bridge evals this way is not a win in the current server path.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not assume the public single-sync idea transfers directly to the current bridge structure; continue looking for structural target-path mismatches rather than local eval reshaping alone.
asi.learning: In the current hybrid bridge, grouped pre-eval of sampled tokens plus selected hidden states is slower than the existing per-array conversion path.

description: Bypass RequestOutputCollector and RequestStreamState entirely for non-streaming generate() requests, storing only the final RequestOutput behind an event. This did not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The remaining non-streaming engine overhead may come from per-request collector/state setup and final drain logic even after finish-only scheduler outputs, so a direct final-output path could help.
asi.rollback_reason: Full-server benchmark throughput stayed below the kept cached-mask baseline, so the direct non-streaming final-output path is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the current finish-only non-streaming path and continue prioritizing larger structural target/runtime differences over more engine-core cleanup.
asi.learning: Collector/state setup for non-streaming generate() requests is not a dominant remaining cost on the current workload.

description: Return one pre-concatenated context-feature tensor from the MLX target bridge instead of converting each selected hidden state separately and concatenating them later in torch. This still failed to beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The hybrid bridge may still be paying unnecessary MLX->torch conversion overhead on selected hidden states; concatenating in MLX and converting once could reduce bridge cost.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this bridge simplification is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Stop spending more time on local hidden-state conversion reshaping unless profiling reveals a dominant bridge hotspot again; prioritize larger target-path differences.
asi.learning: Per-layer hidden-state conversion is not the main remaining limiter in the current hybrid path.

description: Add a dedicated no-parser, non-streaming hot branch in Scheduler._process_batch_responses() so ordinary generate() requests skip the streaming/parser scaffolding on every token. This still regressed the benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Even after finish-only outputs, non-streaming requests may still be paying avoidable per-token branching in Scheduler._process_batch_responses(); an explicit hot branch could reduce that overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The full-server benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this scheduler split-path change is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Prune this scheduler-branch idea and keep focusing on structural target-path differences rather than more response-shaping cleanup.
asi.learning: Per-token parser/stream-branch overhead in Scheduler._process_batch_responses() is not a dominant limiter for the current workload.

description: Precompute a set for target_layer_ids in the MLX target bridge so hidden-state capture uses O(1) membership checks during each verify loop. This did not improve the benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The manual MLX target loop still checks target-layer membership for every layer on every verify pass; replacing tuple membership with a precomputed set might trim some Python overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The primary benchmark stayed well below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this per-layer membership cleanup is not a meaningful win.
asi.next_action_hint: Stop chasing tiny target-bridge Python cleanups unless profiling points back there; focus on larger target-path behavior mismatches.
asi.learning: Per-layer target-layer membership checks are not a meaningful part of the remaining DFlash cost.

description: Keep stream_interval=8 but reduce the non-streaming finish-only engine-loop yield frequency from every 8 steps to every 16 steps, isolating event-loop yielding from token batching. This regressed the benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Some of the kept win may come from the reduced-yield cadence itself rather than stream_interval, and yielding even less often could trim scheduler overhead further on the single-request non-streaming workload.
asi.rollback_reason: The primary benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask baseline, so a looser 16-step yield cadence is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the current 8-step non-streaming yield cadence and deprioritize further event-loop cadence tuning unless profiling exposes a new async bottleneck.
asi.learning: The current 8-step finish-only yield cadence remains better than a 16-step variant on the real server workload.

description: Remove the hybrid DFlash loop's unused position_ids allocation and per-step slicing, since the current MLX target bridge ignores position_ids entirely. This still did not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The hybrid-native loop is doing dead work by building and slicing position_ids tensors that the MLX target bridge never consumes; removing that overhead might improve the real server path.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so this dead-work cleanup is not a meaningful win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep looking for larger structural target-path differences; tiny bookkeeping removals in the hybrid loop are not moving the primary metric enough.
asi.learning: Unused position_ids construction/slicing was not a significant contributor to the current hybrid-path cost.

description: Memoize tokenizer.apply_chat_template() results inside BatchedEngine for identical messages/tools/template kwargs within a server lifetime. This did not improve the primary benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The benchmark repeatedly hits deterministic chat-template rendering on warmup and measured requests, so engine-local memoization might trim legitimate request-path overhead without changing semantics.
asi.rollback_reason: The full benchmark remained below the kept cached-mask baseline, so chat-template memoization is not a meaningful win on this workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Deprioritize chat-template caching and keep focusing on structural DFlash target-path differences rather than frontend request formatting overhead.
asi.learning: Chat-template rendering is not a dominant remaining cost for the current warmed single-request benchmark.

description: Reintroduce an opt-in MLX-native target loop using the corrected Qwen3.5/Omni semantics discovered from the hybrid-vs-native trace: start indexes the already-sampled first token, draft cache crops at the current start, and target cache trims to the new start after the bonus token. This recovers most native-path loss but still does not beat the kept hybrid baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The earlier native target-loop regressions were partly caused by incorrect Qwen3.5/Omni bookkeeping rather than an inherent native-path ceiling; restoring the hybrid-matching start/crop/trim semantics should improve the real server benchmark.
asi.rollback_reason: Even with corrected semantics, the full server benchmark stayed below the kept cached-mask hybrid baseline, so this native target-loop variant is not yet a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the corrected semantic findings as structured memory and continue tracing later-step divergence/correctness in the native loop rather than promoting this path into the default runtime.
asi.learning: A corrected native target loop can climb back to about 28.08 tok/s, which is materially better than the earlier broken native path and confirms the semantic fixes matter, but it is still not enough to beat the kept hybrid path.
asi.native_loop_learning: The critical Qwen3.5/Omni-specific fixes were: start points at the first sampled token in the block, draft cache crops at current start, and target cache trims to the new start after acceptance plus bonus token. With those fixes, the native loop matches the hybrid path across the first several decode steps and regains much of the lost throughput.

description: Reapply the corrected native target loop, but avoid rescanning the nested Qwen3.5 target cache each decode step by tracking target cache length directly in the loop. This regressed the full benchmark and significantly worsened warmup.

asi.hypothesis: Once the native target-loop semantics are corrected, one of the remaining native-only costs is Python cache-length scanning on every decode step; tracking current target length directly could recover enough overhead to beat the hybrid path.
asi.rollback_reason: The primary metric regressed and warmup became much slower, so this tracked-cache-length variant is not correct or not beneficial in the current native loop.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the corrected semantic findings, but do not replace cache-length scanning with this simplistic tracked-length logic; continue debugging later-step native-loop behavior with correctness-first traces.
asi.learning: The native target loop is sensitive to target-cache bookkeeping details beyond just end-of-step trim semantics; a naive tracked-length replacement can degrade both warmup and steady-state throughput.
asi.native_loop_learning: The corrected native-target reconstruction still needs exact target-cache accounting. Matching the hybrid path across many steps is possible, but shortcutting cache-length bookkeeping this way is not safe.

description: Restore the corrected MLX-native target-loop path behind DFLASH_NATIVE_TARGET_LOOP=1 using the long-step trace-matching Qwen3.5/Omni semantics: start at the sampled first token, exact draft-cache crop at current start, and trim the target cache after acceptance plus bonus token. In the integrated server runtime this still stays below the kept cached-mask hybrid baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The main remaining native-loop loss was due to reverted runtime wiring rather than fundamental native-path cost; restoring the exact semantics proven by the long hybrid-vs-native trace should recover the real server path.
asi.rollback_reason: The integrated native target-loop path still benchmarked below the kept hybrid baseline, so it is not yet ready to replace or augment the default DFlash runtime.
asi.next_action_hint: Compare the integrated native loop against the standalone long-step trace harness for hidden-state patching, stop handling, and output assembly differences; then probe correctness/parity directly on full decoded continuations before chasing more speed tweaks.
asi.learning: Long-step trace parity with the hybrid path is necessary but not sufficient for a server-path win. There is still some integrated-runtime difference or residual native-path cost keeping this version under the kept hybrid baseline.
asi.native_loop_learning: Restoring the trace-matching semantics yields a high-27 tok/s native path, but not a win over e7b14c4. The next leverage is in reconciling integrated runtime behavior with the standalone trace harness, not in more cache-length micro-optimizations.

description: Add an explicit post-load Qwen3.5 hidden-states patch so the MLX-native target loop owns per-layer hidden-state capture instead of relying on ad hoc tracing behavior, then rerun the native target-loop path behind DFLASH_NATIVE_TARGET_LOOP=1. This did not improve the integrated server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Part of the remaining native-loop gap was that the standalone trace harness had explicit Qwen3.5 hidden-state capture while the integrated runtime did not own that behavior cleanly; making hidden-state capture a first-class post-load patch might align runtime semantics and recover throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: The native target-loop benchmark still regressed relative to the kept hybrid baseline, so this patch alone is not the missing ingredient for a server-path win.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep using explicit hidden-state capture as a debugging/control mechanism when tracing native-loop correctness, but focus the next experiment on end-to-end continuation parity and acceptance behavior versus the kept hybrid path rather than assuming hidden-state capture wiring is the main bottleneck.
asi.learning: Owning Qwen3.5 hidden-state capture explicitly is useful for native-loop debugging, but it does not by itself eliminate the native server-path gap. The remaining loss is elsewhere in runtime behavior or in native-path cost, not just in hidden-state patching.
asi.hidden_state_patch_learning: The live mlx-lm Qwen3_5TextModel does not retain per-layer hidden states by default. Adding that capability is viable, but it is a correctness/debugging aid rather than an immediate throughput win.

description: Add an opt-in correctness-first hybrid variant that rebuilds the target cache from the accepted prefix before every verify step instead of relying on replay+trim rollback. This confirms the target-cache rollback issue is real, but the end-to-end benchmark still does not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The main parity bug on omni9b-phase2prime is that replaying a speculative block and trimming the target cache is not equivalent to a fresh sequential cache on the accepted prefix; rebuilding the target cache before each verify should restore correctness and may improve acceptance enough to offset the extra work.
asi.rollback_reason: Even though this path targets a real correctness issue, it still benchmarked below the kept baseline on the primary metric, so it is not ready to keep as the serving path.
asi.next_action_hint: Measure continuation parity and acceptance on this rebuild-cache path directly, then look for a cheaper way to reconstruct the correct target state than full-prefix replay each step.
asi.learning: For omni9b-phase2prime, fresh sequential target cache state and replay+trim cache state diverge after the first speculative block. Rebuilding the target cache each verify step is a viable correctness control and does not completely destroy throughput, which makes target-state reconstruction the most promising structural direction.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: The wrong token after the first divergence comes from the target verifier itself, not from accepting a bad draft token. Fresh cache predicts the stock next token while replay+trim predicts the wrong one.

description: Add an opt-in hybrid variant that rebuilds the target cache only on rejection steps, using replay+trim only when a block fully accepts. This targets the observed OmniCoder/Qwen3.5 rollback bug more selectively than rebuilding every verify, but it still does not beat the kept baseline on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: If the target-state mismatch only matters on partial-accept/rejection steps, then rebuilding the target cache selectively on rejection should preserve correctness while avoiding most of the cost of rebuilding every verify.
asi.rollback_reason: The selective rebuild-on-rejection path still benchmarked below the kept hybrid baseline, so it is not a throughput win yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Use the selective rebuild path as a correctness probe: measure acceptance and continuation parity directly, then search for a cheaper way to reconstruct only the broken target-cache components rather than replaying the whole prefix.
asi.learning: The target-cache rollback bug is not limited to a one-off corner case; even a selective correctness-preserving workaround did not recover enough throughput to win. The next structural opportunity is partial target-state reconstruction, not more bridge micro-optimizations.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: Fresh cache reconstruction on the accepted prefix is the right correctness control, but replaying the entire accepted prefix—even only on rejection steps—still costs too much to become the winning serving path.

description: Add an opt-in hybrid variant that snapshots the pre-verify MLX target cache, then on partial acceptance restores that snapshot and replays only the accepted span instead of replaying the whole prefix or relying on replay+trim rollback. This preserves the structural correctness fix more cheaply, but still does not beat the kept baseline on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: The real rollback bug is in partial-token trim of the Qwen3.5/Omni linear ArraysCache layers; restoring the pre-verify cache and replaying only the accepted span should reconstruct the correct target state much more cheaply than rebuilding the entire accepted prefix.
asi.rollback_reason: Even with the cheaper accepted-span replay reconstruction, the primary throughput metric remained below the kept hybrid baseline, so this variant is not yet a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Use accepted-span replay as the main correctness control for rollback semantics, then target the real bottleneck directly: make linear-layer cache rollback exact without replay, or identify a smaller subset of target state that must be reconstructed.
asi.learning: The wrong post-rejection target state is indeed localized enough that replaying only the accepted span from a pre-verify cache snapshot restores correctness in standalone traces. That is a stronger structural result than full-prefix rebuild, but it still needs a cheaper implementation path to become a throughput win.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: The dominant rollback mismatch appears to live in the Qwen3.5/Omni linear ArraysCache layers. Pre-verify snapshot + accepted-span replay is a valid correctness strategy and outperforms full-prefix rebuild conceptually, but not yet enough in the real server path.

description: Refine the accepted-span replay rollback fix by skipping hidden-state capture on the full verify pass when we will discard that verify state anyway, and only collect conditioning hidden states from the accepted-span replay. This is the closest rollback-correct variant so far, but it still stays below the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Accepted-span replay is the right rollback correction, and some of its remaining cost is wasted verify-side hidden-state capture on partial-accept steps; dropping that unused work should recover throughput while preserving correctness.
asi.rollback_reason: This improved on the earlier rollback-correct variants but still did not beat the kept e7b14c4 baseline on the primary metric.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue optimizing the accepted-span replay path: target cache snapshot cost, unnecessary tensor cloning, and only replaying the minimal state needed for the linear ArraysCache layers look like the best remaining structural opportunities.
asi.learning: Skipping verify hidden-state capture on replayed partial-accept steps helps; this is the best rollback-correct server-path variant so far at ~28.17 tok/s. The rollback fix is getting close enough that the remaining gap may be in the snapshot/replay implementation overhead rather than in the core idea.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: Pre-verify snapshot + accepted-span replay remains the strongest correctness-preserving rollback strategy discovered so far. It is now near stock-parity speed, which makes further optimization of that path more promising than the older full-prefix rebuild workaround.

description: Add an opt-in rollback-correct hybrid variant that snapshots only the linear ArraysCache layers before verify, recovers pre-verify attention state by trimming the post-verify attention caches back to the step start, and then replays the accepted span. This preserves parity in standalone traces but still does not beat the kept baseline in the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: Since the main rollback mismatch is concentrated in the linear ArraysCache layers, we can reduce accepted-span replay overhead by snapshotting only linear caches and reconstructing attention state from exact post-verify trims.
asi.rollback_reason: Even though the mixed rollback reconstruction preserves parity in standalone traces, it still stayed below the kept hybrid baseline on the primary metric.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep accepted-span replay as the rollback correctness control, but continue trimming its implementation cost elsewhere; the linear-only snapshot variant did not outperform the fuller replay variant in the real server path.
asi.learning: The rollback bug really is concentrated in the linear ArraysCache layers, but reducing the up-front snapshot scope alone was not enough to produce a server-path win. Some of the remaining cost is still elsewhere in the accepted-span replay machinery.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: Linear-snapshot plus trimmed-attention reconstruction is sufficient for parity in traces, which strengthens the diagnosis. However, the best real benchmark so far still comes from the fuller accepted-span replay path with skipped wasted verify hidden-state capture.

description: Tighten the accepted-span replay rollback fix by reusing the pre-verify cloned MLX cache directly during restore instead of cloning it again before replay. This keeps the rollback-correct path near parity but does not improve on the earlier best variant.

asi.hypothesis: The accepted-span replay path still spends avoidable time cloning the same MLX cache twice; reusing the first clone directly during restore should shave enough overhead to improve the rollback-correct server path.
asi.rollback_reason: The single-clone accepted-span replay path still stayed below the kept baseline and even below the earlier best rollback-correct variant, so this simplification is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Return to the earlier best rollback-correct variant as the control. The next likely savings are not from the second clone alone; focus on where replay-path time is actually spent or on narrowing replay to the minimal target-state reconstruction needed.
asi.learning: The accepted-span replay path is close enough that small implementation changes matter, but removing the second cache clone alone is not the missing win. The major remaining cost is elsewhere in snapshot/replay or in the low-acceptance speculative workload itself.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: Pre-verify snapshot + accepted-span replay remains valid, but naive clone-count reduction is insufficient. The rollback-correct path still needs a more meaningful reduction in reconstruction work to beat the kept baseline.

description: Run the rollback-correct accepted-span replay path with a smaller speculative block size of 8 to reduce per-reject replay cost, now that profiling shows replay dominates and partial rejection happens on almost every step. This still did not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: With rollback-correct replay happening on most steps, the old block_size=16 setting may now be too expensive; reducing block size to 8 could lower replay waste enough to improve real server throughput on the corrected path.
asi.rollback_reason: The smaller block-size rollback-correct path still stayed below the kept baseline, so this is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: The next lever is likely not block size alone. Focus on eliminating or approximating accepted-span replay itself, since replay cost dominates and fires on most steps.
asi.learning: The corrected rollback path changes the optimization landscape, but simply shrinking speculative block size to 8 does not recover enough replay overhead to beat the baseline.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: On the rollback-correct path, replay cost and rejection frequency remain the main bottlenecks. Smaller blocks did not solve that problem on the real server workload.

description: Teach the MLX target bridge to skip sampled-token computation on accepted-span replay, then use that no-sampling replay path in the rollback-correct hybrid variant. This trims replay-side work slightly but still does not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: A meaningful fraction of accepted-span replay cost is wasted in bridge-side lm_head/argmax and MLX->torch sampled-token conversion that the replay path never consumes; skipping that work should improve the rollback-correct server path.
asi.rollback_reason: The no-sampling replay path remained below the kept baseline and did not outperform the earlier best rollback-correct variant.
asi.next_action_hint: The replay bottleneck is not dominated by sampled-token extraction. The next structural step remains exact linear-cache rollback or another way to avoid replay entirely.
asi.learning: Accepted-span replay overhead is not mainly in bridge-side sampled-token extraction. The expensive part is still the target forward/reconstruction itself.
asi.rollback_cache_learning: No-sampling replay is a safe micro-optimization, but it is too small to matter while replay dominates and fires on most steps.
