description: Run a rollback-correct adaptive hybrid: use accepted-span replay DFlash only for the first 128 generated tokens where acceptance is relatively higher, then fall back to plain greedy generation on the corrected target cache for the low-acceptance tail. This is the best adaptive-correctness variant so far, but it still does not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Corrected DFlash is most useful early in the decode, while later low-acceptance steps mostly add replay overhead; switching to plain greedy after the high-acceptance region could outperform both all-DFlash and all-greedy on the benchmark workload.
asi.rollback_reason: The adaptive cutoff improved on the rollback-correct path but still stayed below the kept baseline, so it is not a primary-metric win.
asi.next_action_hint: Treat decode-phase acceptance collapse as a real benchmark characteristic. If pursuing adaptive policies further, optimize the cutoff rule or acceptance-based fallback logic rather than a fixed 128-token switch.
asi.learning: Acceptance on the rollback-correct path is materially better early than late on the 256-token benchmark prompt, and a fixed early-DFlash/late-greedy policy helps somewhat. However, the gain is still not enough to beat the kept baseline.
asi.acceptance_learning: On the benchmark prompt, rollback-correct DFlash averages about 2.85 accepted tokens/step in the first half of decode but only about 1.11 in the second half. Late-phase acceptance collapse is a real limit on end-to-end throughput for this workload.

description: Run the rollback-correct adaptive hybrid with a 96-token speculative cutoff instead of 128, using accepted-span replay for the early decode and plain greedy on the corrected cache afterward. This cutoff is too early and regresses relative to the 128-token adaptive variant.

asi.hypothesis: Because acceptance collapses later in the benchmark decode, switching from corrected DFlash to plain greedy a bit earlier might improve end-to-end throughput further than the previous 128-token cutoff.
asi.rollback_reason: The 96-token cutoff reduced throughput materially, so it is too aggressive for this workload and not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: If pursuing adaptive fallback further, search around the later cutoff region or use an acceptance-based rule rather than an earlier fixed switch.
asi.learning: The adaptive-fallback idea still has some merit, but the useful speculative window on this workload extends past 96 generated tokens. The earlier cutoff leaves too much potential speedup on the table.
asi.acceptance_learning: A fixed speculative cutoff of 96 tokens is too short even with late-phase acceptance collapse; the rollback-correct path still contributes enough benefit between 96 and 128 tokens to matter.

description: Run the rollback-correct adaptive hybrid with a later 160-token speculative cutoff before falling back to plain greedy on the corrected target cache. This is materially worse than the 128-token cutoff, so the useful speculative window does not extend that far on the benchmark workload.

asi.hypothesis: If the fixed 128-token cutoff is switching to greedy slightly too early, pushing the speculative region later to 160 tokens might preserve more of the early DFlash benefit without paying too much late-phase replay cost.
asi.rollback_reason: The 160-token cutoff regressed badly, so extending the speculative region deeper into the low-acceptance tail is not beneficial on this workload.
asi.next_action_hint: If continuing fixed-cutoff sweeps, search closer to the 128-token region rather than later. Acceptance-based fallback may still be more promising than another wide fixed-cutoff jump.
asi.learning: The adaptive speculative window is bounded on both sides: 96 is too early and 160 is too late. The best fixed-cutoff region appears to be somewhere near 128 tokens for the current workload.
asi.acceptance_learning: Late-phase acceptance collapse hurts enough that keeping rollback-correct DFlash active through 160 generated tokens is clearly too expensive.

description: Refine the rollback-correct adaptive hybrid around the current best fixed cutoff by switching from corrected DFlash to plain greedy at 136 generated tokens. This remains below the earlier 128-token cutoff result and below the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The best fixed speculative cutoff might lie near 128 but not exactly on it; moving the adaptive handoff slightly later to 136 tokens could recover a bit more early-DFlash benefit without paying too much low-acceptance tail cost.
asi.rollback_reason: The 136-token cutoff still underperformed the earlier 128-token adaptive variant and did not beat the kept baseline.
asi.next_action_hint: Deprioritize further fixed-cutoff sweeps unless an acceptance-based trigger suggests a narrower window. The fixed-cutoff optimum appears to be near 128 and still below the baseline.
asi.learning: The adaptive-cutoff surface is fairly flat but does not cross the baseline in the nearby region tested so far. 128 remains the best fixed cutoff tried, with 96/136/160 all worse.
asi.acceptance_learning: A slightly later fixed switch at 136 does not help, which suggests the remaining gain is not in simple cutoff tuning but in a more responsive acceptance-based fallback or better rollback economics.

description: Add an acceptance-triggered adaptive fallback on top of the rollback-correct accepted-span replay path: after at least 64 generated tokens, stop speculative decoding once the last 8 speculative steps average below 1.75 accepted tokens and finish with plain greedy generation on the corrected target cache. This did not beat the earlier best fixed cutoff or the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: A rolling acceptance-based fallback should outperform fixed cutoffs by switching from rollback-correct DFlash to plain greedy near the real point where speculative usefulness collapses on a given prompt.
asi.rollback_reason: The first acceptance-triggered rule still stayed below both the best fixed 128-token cutoff and the kept cached-mask hybrid baseline.
asi.next_action_hint: If continuing adaptive policies, tune around stricter/later fallback thresholds or shorter windows, but do not spend too many more cycles here unless one variant clearly surpasses the fixed-cutoff control.
asi.learning: Acceptance-triggered fallback is viable to test but not automatically better than a tuned fixed cutoff. This first rule likely falls back too early or too noisily for the benchmark workload.
asi.acceptance_learning: A rule of window=8, threshold=1.75, min_tokens=64 underperformed the simpler 128-token cutoff. The adaptive trigger needs tighter calibration if it is going to help.

description: Tune the acceptance-triggered adaptive fallback later and tighter: after at least 96 generated tokens, stop speculative decoding only when the last 6 speculative steps average below 1.5 accepted tokens, then finish with plain greedy generation on the corrected target cache. This still does not beat the best fixed cutoff or the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The earlier adaptive fallback likely switched too early; delaying the minimum speculative region and requiring a tighter rolling-acceptance collapse should approximate the useful 128-token fixed cutoff more closely while remaining prompt-adaptive.
asi.rollback_reason: Even with a later and tighter trigger, the adaptive rule still underperformed the fixed-cutoff control and the kept baseline.
asi.next_action_hint: Deprioritize more acceptance-trigger tuning unless a very targeted rule is motivated by traces. The adaptive-policy path is not obviously outperforming the simpler fixed cutoff.
asi.learning: Later/tighter rolling-acceptance fallback remains close to the earlier adaptive result but does not improve it. The adaptive-policy surface appears fairly flat and still below the fixed 128-token cutoff.
asi.acceptance_learning: A window=6, threshold=1.5, min_tokens=96 trigger is not enough to beat the fixed 128-token cutoff. Acceptance-triggered fallback is not currently delivering extra value over simple fixed scheduling.

description: Add an opt-in sequential-verify hybrid path that feeds the speculative block through the target one token at a time, stopping at first mismatch so the target cache naturally ends at the accepted span with no rollback or replay. This makes low acceptance less pathological and is competitive, but it still does not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Because rollback-correct replay is expensive and low acceptance is common, a token-by-token verify path that stops at first mismatch could turn low acceptance into a compute savings instead of paying full-block verify plus replay overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The sequential-verify path remained below the kept cached-mask hybrid baseline on the primary metric, so it is not a win yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Validate continuation parity on the sequential-verify path; if correct, it is a useful alternative control because it avoids rollback complexity entirely. Then profile where its remaining cost lives before abandoning it.
asi.learning: Sequential verify is more competitive than many of the replay-based correctness fixes and avoids the linear-cache rollback problem by construction. Its remaining gap is now about efficiency, not obvious correctness debt.
asi.verify_learning: With low acceptance, token-by-token verification can be viable. It does not automatically beat the baseline, but it is close enough to justify parity checking and targeted profiling.

description: Combine the parity-correct sequential-verify hybrid path with a 128-token adaptive handoff to plain greedy generation on the existing target cache. This underperformed plain sequential verify and did not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: If sequential verify is correct and competitive but suffers from late low-acceptance overhead, the best fixed-cutoff idea might transfer and improve it by switching to plain greedy for the second half of the decode.
asi.rollback_reason: Sequential verify plus a 128-token handoff still stayed below both the kept baseline and the no-cutoff sequential-verify control, so this combination is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not spend more time mixing fixed cutoffs into sequential verify unless a profile suggests a very specific late-phase bottleneck. Sequential verify’s remaining cost likely needs different treatment.
asi.learning: Sequential verify is not obviously improved by the same fixed-cutoff strategy that marginally helped the replay-based rollback-correct path. Its cost structure appears different enough that the simple transfer does not help.
asi.verify_learning: The parity-correct sequential-verify path remains a useful control, but fixed late fallback did not improve it. The remaining gap likely sits in per-step bridge/target call overhead rather than just late acceptance collapse.

description: Run the parity-correct sequential-verify hybrid path with a smaller speculative block size of 8. With low acceptance, shrinking the drafted block reduces wasted draft work enough to nearly tie the kept baseline, but this run still did not beat it.

asi.hypothesis: On the sequential-verify path, low acceptance makes large drafted blocks wasteful; reducing block_size from 16 to 8 could fit the real acceptance regime better and close the remaining throughput gap.
asi.rollback_reason: This run came very close but still remained slightly below the kept e7b14c4 baseline, so it is not a primary-metric win yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Because this result is near-tied, consider a confirmation rerun of the same sequential-verify block_size=8 variant before abandoning it. If it repeatedly stays just under the baseline, then it is a useful correctness control but not a keep.
asi.learning: Sequential verify plus block_size=8 is the strongest sequential-verify variant so far and one of the closest correctness-preserving paths to the kept baseline. The sequential path appears more compatible with smaller blocks than the older replay-based fixes.
asi.verify_learning: On the parity-correct sequential-verify path, smaller speculative blocks help materially. That suggests its remaining cost is sensitive to draft overproduction rather than just target-call overhead.

description: Confirmation rerun for the parity-correct sequential-verify hybrid with block_size=8. The first near-tie did not reproduce; this rerun came in lower and confirms the variant is still below the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The first sequential-verify block_size=8 result was close enough to the baseline that it might have been a real near-tie worth confirming before dropping.
asi.rollback_reason: The confirmation run came in materially lower, so the apparent near-tie was not stable enough to justify keeping the variant.
asi.next_action_hint: Treat sequential-verify block_size=8 as an interesting but still sub-baseline correctness-preserving control, not a winning serving path. Move on to a different structural lever.
asi.learning: Sequential verify with block_size=8 is not stable at the near-baseline level on rerun. Its earlier closeness was likely noise rather than a reproducible win.
asi.verify_learning: A confirmation rerun matters here: despite one promising sample, the sequential-verify block_size=8 variant still belongs in the discard pile.

description: Implement an opt-in MLX-native target sequential-verify path: run the parity-correct token-by-token verify loop directly against the native Qwen3.5/Omni target instead of the torch bridge, so the target cache naturally stays at the accepted span without rollback or replay. This still does not beat the kept baseline on the full server benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: If sequential verify is the right correctness shape, then moving it onto the native MLX target path should remove bridge overhead and outperform the hybrid sequential-verify variant.
asi.rollback_reason: The native-target sequential-verify path remained below the kept cached-mask hybrid baseline, so it is not a win yet.
asi.next_action_hint: Use native-target sequential verify as another correctness-preserving control, but do not assume bridge removal alone will unlock a win. The remaining bottleneck is likely low acceptance and per-step target work itself.
asi.learning: Native-target sequential verify does not materially outperform the hybrid sequential-verify path on the real workload. That implies the main remaining limit is not just bridge overhead.
asi.verify_learning: Sequential verify is viable on both hybrid and native target paths, but neither path beats the kept baseline. Low acceptance remains the dominant structural problem.

description: Run the parity-correct sequential-verify hybrid path with an even smaller speculative block size of 4. This reduces draft overproduction further and improves on the confirmed block_size=8 sequential result, but it still does not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: If sequential verify is limited by wasted drafted tokens under low acceptance, shrinking the speculative block from 8 to 4 should fit the real acceptance distribution better and improve throughput further.
asi.rollback_reason: Even with block_size=4, the sequential-verify path remained below the kept e7b14c4 baseline, so it is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Sequential verify seems to prefer smaller blocks than the replay-based paths, but the gain still isn’t enough. Unless a confirmation rerun surprises us, the next experiments should move to different structural levers rather than more block-size shrinking.
asi.learning: Sequential verify benefits from smaller blocks, but the improvement from 8 to 4 still stops short of a real win. The path is useful as a correctness-preserving control, not yet as the new serving baseline.
asi.verify_learning: On the parity-correct sequential-verify path, block_size=4 currently outperforms block_size=8, which supports the idea that low-acceptance workloads punish over-large draft blocks.

description: Run the parity-correct sequential-verify hybrid path with a very small speculative block size of 2. This is the strongest sequential-verify result so far and improves on the block_size=4 variant, but it still does not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: If the sequential-verify path prefers ever smaller blocks under low acceptance, reducing the speculative block to 2 may minimize draft overproduction enough to close the remaining throughput gap.
asi.rollback_reason: Even though block_size=2 is the best sequential-verify variant so far, this run still remained below the kept e7b14c4 baseline, so it is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: If this branch stays alive, block_size=2 is now the right sequential-verify control to confirm or profile. Otherwise, move to a larger structural change rather than more block-size nudging.
asi.learning: Sequential verify continues to improve as the speculative block shrinks, which reinforces the idea that over-drafting is its main cost. However, even the smallest practical block tested so far still falls short of the kept baseline.
asi.verify_learning: On the parity-correct sequential-verify path, the ranking is now roughly block_size=2 > 4 > 8 > 16. Smaller blocks fit the observed low-acceptance regime better, but not enough yet to win.

description: Confirmation rerun for the parity-correct sequential-verify hybrid with block_size=2. The first result did not reproduce; this rerun came in lower and confirms the variant is still below the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The first block_size=2 sequential-verify result was close enough to the baseline that it was worth checking whether it represented a stable improvement trend.
asi.rollback_reason: The confirmation run came in lower, so the initial block_size=2 sample was not a reproducible win.
asi.next_action_hint: Treat sequential-verify block_size=2 as another sub-baseline correctness-preserving control and move on to larger structural ideas rather than more block-size tweaks.
asi.learning: Sequential verify with block_size=2 is not a stable near-baseline improvement. The sequential branch still has not produced a reproducible primary-metric win despite better-fitting smaller blocks.
asi.verify_learning: Confirmation reruns matter here too: the apparent improvement trend for smaller sequential blocks is real in direction, but not enough to turn the branch into a winner on this workload.

description: Add an env-gated packed-QKV patch for Qwen3.5/Qwen3Next attention that fuses q_proj/k_proj/v_proj into one matmul, then benchmark it on the stock path first. This regressed throughput in this environment.

asi.hypothesis: One of the remaining public high-signal levers is reducing attention projection dispatch count; packing Q/K/V into a single matmul might raise the target-side speed floor for both stock and corrected DFlash paths.
asi.rollback_reason: The packed-QKV attention patch reduced the primary metric on the stock path, so it is not a win to carry forward into DFlash variants.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not prioritize the packed-QKV attention route in this environment. Return focus to acceptance/correctness structure rather than projection-dispatch count.
asi.learning: The public packed-QKV idea does not transfer as a win here, at least with this straightforward patch. Either the current MLX stack already optimizes the relevant path sufficiently or the added patching overhead outweighs the dispatch reduction.
asi.target_speed_learning: Stock target throughput regressed under the packed-QKV patch, so the main remaining ceiling is unlikely to be raw target Q/K/V projection dispatch count in this setup.

description: Combine the parity-correct sequential-verify path at block_size=2 with a 128-token handoff to plain greedy generation. This clearly regressed, so the fixed-cutoff idea does not transfer to the strongest small-block sequential variant either.

asi.hypothesis: Because block_size=2 is the best sequential-verify fit for the low-acceptance regime so far, combining it with the previously best 128-token cutoff might finally beat the baseline by trimming late-phase speculative overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: The combination regressed badly, so this fixed-cutoff strategy is not compatible with the small-block sequential path on the benchmark workload.
asi.next_action_hint: Drop the sequential-cutoff branch. Move on to larger structural changes rather than combining two individually weak knobs.
asi.learning: The best small-block sequential variant does not benefit from the same fixed-cutoff idea that slightly helped the replay-based path. These two branches do not compose well.
asi.verify_learning: Sequential verify and fixed late fallback are not complementary in this workload. The remaining gap is elsewhere.

description: Override the drafter conditioning layers to a shifted set `(4,10,16,22,28)` that improved acceptance in a standalone corrected-semantic diagnostic, and benchmark it on the strongest sequential-verify branch (`block_size=2`). The acceptance-only improvement did not translate to a server-path win.

asi.hypothesis: Because low acceptance is the main bottleneck now, a conditioning-layer set that improves acceptance under corrected semantics might finally unlock a throughput win when paired with the strongest small-block sequential-verify path.
asi.rollback_reason: Despite looking better in the acceptance diagnostic, the shifted-layer variant regressed on the real server benchmark, so it is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not trust acceptance-only layer changes, even under corrected semantics, without end-to-end benchmarking. The next path should focus on a different structural bottleneck.
asi.learning: The earlier lesson still holds after the rollback/correctness work: better acceptance in a narrow diagnostic does not guarantee better end-to-end throughput. Conditioning-layer changes remain dangerous to optimize by proxy.
asi.acceptance_learning: Even on the strongest corrected sequential branch, the shifted layer set `(4,10,16,22,28)` did not pay off in the real server benchmark.

description: Add a parity-correct sequential-verify path for the official z-lab torch drafter and benchmark it on the real server workload. The torch drafter clearly improved acceptance in diagnostics versus the native MLX drafter, but the end-to-end server path still did not beat the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: Our native MLX drafter port may still be suppressing acceptance materially; if so, the official torch drafter combined with corrected sequential verification should outperform the native sequential branch despite the bridge overhead.
asi.rollback_reason: Even with materially better acceptance in diagnostics, the torch-drafter sequential path still stayed below the kept baseline on the real server benchmark.
asi.next_action_hint: If revisiting this branch, test whether smaller block sizes help the torch sequential path the same way they helped the native sequential path. Otherwise focus on other structural bottlenecks.
asi.learning: Higher acceptance alone was not enough to overcome the end-to-end cost of the torch sequential path in this first benchmark. The bridge/runtime overhead is still substantial.
asi.drafter_learning: The official torch drafter appears stronger than the native MLX port on corrected-semantics acceptance diagnostics, but that strength did not automatically transfer into higher full-server throughput.

description: Benchmark the parity-correct torch-drafter sequential-verify path at block_size=8 after the initial block_size=16 result suggested acceptance might benefit from smaller speculative blocks. It remained below the kept baseline.

asi.hypothesis: The official torch drafter has materially better corrected-semantics acceptance than the native MLX port, and smaller blocks previously helped the native sequential branch, so block_size=8 might be the best torch sequential operating point.
asi.rollback_reason: The torch sequential block_size=8 rerun still stayed below the kept e7b14c4 baseline, so it is not a win.
asi.next_action_hint: Do not spend more time on torch sequential block-size tuning unless a new profile suggests a qualitatively different bottleneck; return to structural drafter/runtime work.
asi.learning: Even with the stronger official torch drafter and a smaller block, the parity-correct sequential server path still does not convert better acceptance into a throughput win.
asi.drafter_learning: The native-vs-torch acceptance gap is real, but bridge/runtime cost remains high enough that simply swapping to the stronger torch drafter is insufficient.

description: Ported an env-gated server path for boundary-snapshot speculative verify plus native-MLX fresh linear-SSM refresh at completion token 168. It restored exact server-path parity on the benchmark prompt, but full benchmark throughput fell to 24.69 tok/s, well below the ~28.36 tok/s baseline, so the refresh/reanchor control is useful only as a correctness probe, not a throughput win.

Retested a parity-correct boundary-snapshot server path that does a native-MLX fresh linear-SSM refresh at completion token 168, then hands off to plain greedy at token 172. It preserved exact benchmark-prompt parity and materially improved over the full-refresh-all-the-way variant, but three real-server runs still stayed below the kept ~28.36 tok/s baseline (28.26, 28.13, 28.16 tok/s), so this late-greedy fallback is a near-miss, not a reproducible win.

Ported a cheaper parity-correct boundary-snapshot server path that refreshes exact linear SSM state from a text-model-only fresh prefix rebuild (embed + transformer stack only), then hands off to greedy at completion token 172. Real benchmark-prompt parity held, but throughput was still only 27.81 tok/s, below the kept ~28.36 tok/s baseline.

Tested a parity-correct boundary-snapshot server path that refreshes exact late linear SSM state from a text-model rebuild with fresh attention-cache writes disabled (only linear caches materialized), then hands off to greedy at completion token 170. Exact benchmark-prompt parity held, but throughput was still only 27.78 tok/s, below the kept ~28.36 tok/s baseline.

Restore DFlash benchmark flag; add env-gated boundary snapshot path with late sparse exact refresh. New candidate uses boundary restore + two late native-text linear-only reanchors at completion tokens 168 and 170, refreshing only linear layer 6 before handoff to greedy at 170. Server reruns stayed above the kept ~28.36 baseline (28.47, 28.40, 28.40, 28.44 tok/s), so keep as a small but consistent win.

Candidate env-only variant: boundary snapshot path with a single late native-text linear-only reanchor at 168 refreshing only layer 6, no greedy handoff. Parity path stayed correct in the probe but full server throughput dropped to 25.64 tok/s, well below the kept dual-reanchor+cutoff variant and below baseline.

Candidate env-only variant: boundary snapshot path with two late linear-only reanchors at 168 and 170, refreshing layers 1 and 18 before greedy handoff at 170. Probe parity held, but full server throughput only reached 27.66 tok/s, below the kept single-layer-6 dual-reanchor variant and below stock baseline.

Candidate env-only variant: kept boundary snapshot schedule (reanchor 168 and 170, layer 6 only, greedy handoff at 170) but stop the fresh native-text rebuild at the highest refreshed layer instead of running the full text stack. This reduced throughput to 28.30 tok/s, so the shorter rebuild path is not a win in the real server path.

Candidate env-only variant: move the first sparse exact refresh one token earlier (reanchors at 167 and 170 instead of 168 and 170) while keeping the single-layer-6 boundary snapshot schedule and greedy handoff at 170. Three server reruns clustered at 28.46, 28.45, and 28.42 tok/s—effectively tied with the kept 168/170 schedule, so discard as no material gain.

Candidate env-only variant: delay the first sparse exact refresh to 169 while keeping the second refresh and greedy handoff at 170 on the single-layer-6 boundary snapshot path. Full server throughput was 28.39 tok/s, effectively tied to or slightly worse than the kept 168/170 schedule, so discard.

Candidate env-only variant: boundary snapshot path with dual reanchors at 168 and 170, refreshing layers 2 and 4 before greedy handoff at 170. This looked slightly better than the single-layer-6 schedule in the standalone probe, but full server throughput collapsed to 25.58 tok/s, so discard.

Env-gated bridge micro-opt: prefer numpy conversion instead of DLPack for small integer MLX->torch token-id tensors on the kept boundary snapshot schedule (layer 6, reanchors 168/170, greedy cutoff 170). Full server throughput was 28.39 tok/s, slightly below the kept path, so discard.

Fix the env-gated Qwen3.5 boundary-capture patch to match the real mlx-lm GatedDeltaNet forward (`self.norm(out, z)` / `out_proj`) instead of the earlier incorrect output-path assumption. Verified exact-match parity against stock on the benchmark prompt with the kept boundary schedule (layer 6, reanchors 168/170, greedy handoff 170). Server reruns after the fix still stayed above the kept ~28.36 baseline (28.40 and 28.38 tok/s), so keep the corrected patch.

name: oMLX bstnxbt-style DFlash long-decode bringup (1024-token exact workload)

description: Port the first bstnxbt-style MLX DFlash path into oMLX serving: add a new `bstnxbt_mlx` backend, recurrent rollback cache with innovation-tape replay semantics, exact-small-projection padding hooks, direct MLX target forward/capture/verify, and oMLX runtime dispatch behind `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1`. Verified exact-match server output against stock on the benchmark prompt at 64, 256, and 1024 completion tokens. On the 1024-token workload this new path reaches 28.20 tok/s, beating the manual stock comparator for the same workload (~26.30 tok/s) while serving through normal oMLX.

Tried porting bstnxbt's draft-side ContextOnlyDraftKVCache / sink+window behavior into the oMLX native drafter on top of the kept bstnxbt-style backend. Exactness was preserved, but real 1024-token server throughput dropped to 27.93 tok/s versus the kept 28.20+ band, so this draft-cache port is not a win in this tree and should stay out.

name: oMLX Qwen3.5-9B official long-decode DFlash bringup

description: Baseline for the new official Qwen3.5-9B oMLX bringup target: restore missing config/tokenizer metadata into `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16/` so oMLX can discover the model, then measure plain stock generation on the 1024-token warmed long-decode workload. This establishes the non-DFlash comparator at 28.34 tok/s before testing the new bstnxbt-style backend on the same official target.

Test the new bstnxbt-style oMLX backend on the official `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` target for the same 1024-token warmed workload. Restored model metadata allowed oMLX discovery and the DFlash path matched stock output exactly through 1024 tokens, but throughput was only 26.66 tok/s versus the official-target stock baseline of 28.34 tok/s. Discard as a correctness-preserving but slower cross-target transfer; the remaining missing wins are still target-side/runtime details, not basic bringup.

Port the rollback path onto the dedicated bstnxbt-style tape-replay-kernel module and re-test the official `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` target on the 1024-token workload. Exact output vs stock was preserved and throughput recovered sharply from the earlier 26.66 tok/s cross-target run to 28.08 tok/s, but that is still below the stock baseline of 28.34 tok/s, so discard for now while keeping the learning that target-side tape-replay details materially matter on official Qwen3.5.

Try the next bstnxbt-style target-side port on the official `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` workload: keep the dedicated tape-replay-kernel module and add the long-context full-attention split verify hook for cached prefixes >=1024. Exactness was preserved, but throughput regressed to 27.72 tok/s from the earlier tape-replay-only 28.08 tok/s and remained below the 28.34 tok/s stock baseline, so discard this naive split-verify port.

Try a targeted bstnxbt-style target optimization on the official `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` path: pack Qwen3Next MLP gate/up projections into a single linear while leaving attention unpacked. Exactness vs stock was preserved, but throughput only reached 27.89 tok/s on the 1024-token workload, still below the 28.34 tok/s stock baseline and below the earlier tape-replay-only 28.08 tok/s result, so discard.

Trim a few explicit sync points in the bstnxbt-style official-target path by evaluating greedy sampled IDs together with the already-needed context tensors instead of forcing separate `mx.eval()` boundaries on logits first. This improved the official Qwen3.5-9B 1024-token run slightly to 28.11 tok/s from the earlier 28.08 tok/s tape-replay-only shape, but it still does not beat the 28.34 tok/s stock baseline, so discard.

Try a more aggressive bstnxbt-style sync-reduction variant on the official Qwen3.5-9B path: evaluate sampled IDs together with context tensors and add `mx.async_eval(draft_logits)` before greedy draft token extraction. First run looked promising at 28.27 tok/s, but confirmation runs collapsed to 27.44 and then 21.50 tok/s, so this async-overlap shape is too unstable to trust and is discarded.

name: bstnxbt dflash-mlx reference validation on local Qwen3.5-9B

description: Attempted to time the external `bstnxbt/dflash-mlx` benchmark with `run_experiment`, but the autoresearch harness correctly rejected the custom command because this repo contains `autoresearch.sh`. No model benchmark ran; switching to plain bash for external-repo validation instead.

name: oMLX Qwen3.5-9B official long-decode DFlash parity port

description: Re-anchor the official `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` oMLX DFlash port target at current HEAD before further bstnxbt parity work. The in-tree `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1` path now measures 28.32 tok/s on the 1024-token workload, essentially back at the prior near-baseline band and close to the 28.34 stock comparator.

Restore the dedicated bstnxbt kernel layer into the in-tree official Qwen3.5-9B path: add `omlx/dflash/bstnxbt_kernels.py`, wire `make_target_cache()` to `tape_replay_kernel`, and use `gated_delta_kernel_with_tape` in `_forward_linear_with_tape()` when speculative tape recording is armed. Exact output parity vs stock still held, but the official 1024-token oMLX workload regressed to 27.62 tok/s from the new 28.32 tok/s baseline, so the missing gap is not solved by the kernel drop-in alone.

Port the external bstnxbt delegation shape into the in-tree official Qwen3.5-9B path: install a speculative `linear_attn.__call__` hook and let `target_forward_with_hidden_states()` use the normal layer forward instead of reimplementing whole linear layers in Python. The first run nearly tied stock at 28.34 tok/s, but the confirmation run fell to 27.95 tok/s, so this is a structurally better match to the reference runtime but not a reliable throughput win yet.

Port the actual bstnxbt long-context full-attention verify path back into oMLX: add `batched_sdpa_2pass_exact`, install the split-full-attention hook on target full-attention layers, and enable it behind `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1`. The first official Qwen3.5-9B run looked promising at 28.60 tok/s and exact-match parity held, but the confirmation run dropped to 27.72 tok/s, below the 28.32 baseline, so discard as non-reproducible on the oMLX server path.

Port the combined external bstnxbt target-runtime shape into oMLX for the official Qwen3.5-9B path: install speculative `linear_attn.__call__` hooks for recurrent rollback, let the normal Qwen3.5 layers run instead of reimplementing full linear layers in Python, and enable the actual long-context full-attention split path with `batched_sdpa_2pass_exact` behind `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1`. Exact-match parity vs stock held on the benchmark prompt, and the official 1024-token oMLX workload improved to 28.77 tok/s from the 28.32 baseline.

On top of the kept combined external-style official-target port, add the recurrent bstnxbt kernel pair: use `gated_delta_kernel_with_tape` while recording speculative linear state and switch rollback replay to `tape_replay_kernel`. The official Qwen3.5-9B 1024-token result reached 28.75 tok/s, effectively flat-to-slightly-worse than the kept 28.77 tok/s combined path, so discard.

Keep the combined external-style official-target port but lower the long-context split-attention activation threshold from 1024 to 512 KV tokens via `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_THRESHOLD=512`. This reached 28.69 tok/s on the official Qwen3.5-9B 1024-token workload, below the kept 28.77 tok/s path, so discard.

On top of the kept combined external-style official-target port, reintroduce the external-style `ContextOnlyDraftKVCache` for the MLX-native drafter via `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_CONTEXT_ONLY_DRAFT_CACHE=1`. Exact-match parity vs stock held, but the official Qwen3.5-9B 1024-token workload reached only 28.48 tok/s, below the kept 28.77 tok/s path, so discard.

Try making the long-context split-attention verify path the default bstnxbt behavior in oMLX by enabling it unless `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA` is explicitly disabled. On the official Qwen3.5-9B 1024-token workload this measured 28.26 tok/s, below both the explicit kept combined path at 28.77 tok/s and the 28.32 baseline, so discard.

name: oMLX OmniCoder long-decode bstnxbt combined-port transfer

description: Start a new OmniCoder 1024-token long-decode transfer segment by measuring the kept official-Qwen combined-port shape on the real Omni target: `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1 DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1` with the normal oMLX server path. This establishes the transferred combined-port result at 28.00 tok/s on `models/omni9b-phase2prime`.

On the new OmniCoder 1024-token transfer segment, retest the current `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1` path without the split-attention env to see if the official-Qwen combined-port win generalizes cleanly. The first sample was slightly above the split-enabled baseline at 28.09 tok/s, but the confirmation run collapsed to 24.49 tok/s, so treat this Omni long-decode branch as unstable/noisy and discard the no-split follow-up.

name: oMLX OmniCoder main 256-token bstnxbt combined-port transfer

description: Re-anchor the real primary target around the current bstnxbt combined-port shape on the honest OmniCoder 256-token main workload: `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1 DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1` through the normal oMLX server path. This establishes the transferred combined-port baseline at 28.02 tok/s on `models/omni9b-phase2prime`.

On the real OmniCoder 256-token main workload, retest the current bstnxbt combined-port code without `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1` to verify whether the long-context split path is adding overhead when it should never trigger below the 1024-KV threshold. Result was effectively flat-to-worse at 27.99 tok/s versus the 28.02 baseline, so discard.

name: oMLX Qwen3.5-9B official long-decode DFlash to 60 tok/s

description: Re-anchor the loop on the agreed target again: official `mlx_models/Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` through the oMLX server, using the current kept combined bstnxbt-style path (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1 DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1`). This re-establishes the active Qwen long-decode baseline at 28.34 tok/s before further parity-port work toward the 60 tok/s goal.

Retest bstnxbt-style target MLP packing only on the agreed target and current kept combined path: `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_PACK_MLP=1` together with `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1 DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1` on official `Qwen3.5-9B-bf16` through the normal oMLX server. Throughput regressed to 27.33 tok/s versus the 28.34 baseline, so discard.

Port one concrete external-style hot-loop delta into the kept combined official-Qwen path: replace Python-list token accumulation with preallocated MLX generated/block token buffers in `execute_bstnxbt_mlx_generate(...)`, while keeping `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1 DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1` on the normal oMLX server path. Throughput regressed to 27.56 tok/s versus the 28.34 baseline, so discard.

name: oMLX Qwen3.5-9B external-style no-EOS DFlash to 60 tok/s

description: Tried to re-anchor the new external-style official-Qwen target using an env-prefixed `run_experiment` command. The harness rejected it because `autoresearch.sh` exists and requires being invoked directly, so this attempt is a command-shape crash rather than a model/runtime failure.

Re-anchor the active target on the external-style official-Qwen workload through the normal oMLX server: official `Qwen3.5-9B-bf16`, math prompt matching the external reference shape, 1024-token decode, `ignore_eos=true`, and current combined bstnxbt path via `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RUNTIME=1 DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1`. Current oMLX DFlash baseline on this workload is 26.97 tok/s.

Aligned external-style official-Qwen workload follow-up: drop `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=1` while keeping the rest of the combined bstnxbt path the same (`ignore_eos=true`, math prompt, 1024 tokens through normal oMLX server). First run improved to 27.29 tok/s, but a mandatory confirmation rerun fell back to 26.96 tok/s, so treat the no-split result as noise and discard.

Confirmation rerun of the aligned external-style official-Qwen no-split variant (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=0`) came in at 26.96 tok/s, effectively matching/slightly missing the 26.97 split baseline. This confirms the earlier 27.29 sample was not a reliable improvement.

Aligned external-style official-Qwen retest of the external draft-side `ContextOnlyDraftKVCache` (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_CONTEXT_ONLY_DRAFT_CACHE=1`) on top of the current combined bstnxbt path. First run improved to 27.51 tok/s, but later reruns proved the variant unstable, so discard rather than trust the initial sample.

Confirmation rerun of aligned external-style official-Qwen with `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_CONTEXT_ONLY_DRAFT_CACHE=1` reached 28.17 tok/s, but a third rerun collapsed to 19.95 tok/s. Despite the strong middle sample, the variant is not stable enough to keep.
Third rerun of the aligned external-style official-Qwen `ContextOnlyDraftKVCache` variant collapsed to 19.95 tok/s, confirming severe instability on this server path. Discard the variant.

Aligned external-style official-Qwen parity test: make `ignore_eos=true` behave more like external `dflash-mlx` by suppressing EOS during greedy sampling inside the bstnxbt DFlash loop, not just removing EOS from stop tokens. First two runs improved to 27.17 and 27.62 tok/s versus the 26.97 baseline, but a third confirmation run collapsed to 23.67 tok/s, so treat the patch as unstable and discard.

Restore the external bstnxbt recurrent tape-kernel path in-tree and enable it on the aligned external-style official-Qwen workload via `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RECURRENT_KERNELS=1`, while keeping the current combined split-SDPA bstnxbt server path. On the exact active math/no-EOS 1024-token workload this improved oMLX from the 26.97 baseline to 28.10 tok/s, so the remaining large gap is now more clearly in other runtime-port differences, not this recurrent rollback layer.

Stack the external-style no-EOS sampling semantics back on top of the kept recurrent-kernel path: when `ignore_eos=true`, suppress EOS from greedy token selection inside the bstnxbt DFlash loop instead of only omitting EOS from stop tokens. On the exact aligned official-Qwen math/no-EOS workload this nudged throughput from 28.10 to 28.23 tok/s while matching the external runtime shape more closely, so keep the parity fix.

Replace the old custom native drafter with an in-tree copy of the external bstnxbt draft-model implementation and load it automatically for the bstnxbt oMLX runtime, keeping recurrent kernels + split SDPA + no-EOS suppression on the aligned official-Qwen workload. Despite a strong direct-loop diagnostic on a raw prompt, the real oMLX server benchmark regressed to 27.73 tok/s versus the kept 28.23 path, so discard; the draft-side acceptance gain did not transfer honestly to the chat-templated server workload.

Wire the exact external bstnxbt draft model into the oMLX server behind `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_EXTERNAL_DRAFT=1`, keeping recurrent kernels + split SDPA + no-EOS suppression on the aligned official-Qwen workload. Despite strong direct-loop diagnostics, the real oMLX server benchmark fell hard to 24.94 tok/s, so discard; naive external-draft injection is not enough and likely fights the server/runtime integration shape.

Port the missing external-style chunked verify path into the in-tree bstnxbt runtime and test it honestly on the aligned official-Qwen workload via `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_VERIFY_CHUNK_TOKENS=8`, which disables speculative linear rollback caches and verifies the target block in smaller chunks. This regressed to 27.88 tok/s versus the kept 28.23 path, so discard; chunked verify is not the next win here.

Port the remaining external target-side packing delta that was still missing in the live tree: env-gated Qwen3Next attention QKV packing via `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_PACK_ATTENTION=1`, while keeping the aligned official-Qwen path on split SDPA + recurrent kernels + EOS suppression. This regressed to 27.51 tok/s versus the kept 28.23 path, so discard; target attention packing is not the missing server-path win here.

Port more of bstnxbt’s current outer generate-loop shape into the live in-tree runtime while keeping the aligned official-Qwen workload unchanged: add `DFLASH_VERIFY_LEN` support, prefix-aware rollback arming, reusable token buffers, and `mx.async_eval` on draft logits. The honest server benchmark regressed to 27.81 tok/s versus the kept 28.23 path, and follow-up in-process diagnostics showed no meaningful external-draft runtime improvement either, so discard; the remaining gap is not just the outer loop shape.

Port external-style context-only draft-cache semantics into the native MLX drafter behind `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_CONTEXT_ONLY_DRAFT_CACHE=1`: add an in-tree `ContextOnlyDraftKVCache`, teach native draft attention to use `append_context/fetch`, and let native `make_cache()` emit context-only caches when env-gated. This was a strong direct runtime win on the exact aligned prompt (repeated `DFlashRuntime.generate(...)` stabilized around 31.7–32.9 tok/s with acceptance ratio 0.8662 and 137 cycles), and the first honest server run improved to 28.74 tok/s. But the confirmation `./autoresearch.sh` rerun dropped to 27.76 tok/s, below the kept 28.23 path, so discard for now; the draft-runtime semantics look much better, but the full server lifecycle still cancels the gain.

name: Aligned official-Qwen actual DFlash server benchmark after greedy request fix

description: Fix the HTTP benchmark so DFlash requests are actually greedy and no longer silently fall back to stock generation: `scripts/siliconflash_benchmark.py` now sends `top_p=1`, `top_k=0`, and `min_p=0.0` alongside `temperature=0`, with tests updated to lock that contract. This starts a fresh corrected-benchmark segment and establishes the first honest aligned official-Qwen server-path DFlash baseline at 37.38 tok/s.

Re-try the strongest real draft-side lead on the corrected greedy HTTP benchmark: add env-gated external-style context-only draft-cache semantics to the native MLX drafter. This ports an in-tree `ContextOnlyDraftKVCache`, teaches native draft attention to use `append_context/fetch`, and lets native `make_cache()` emit context-only caches when `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_CONTEXT_ONLY_DRAFT_CACHE=1`. On the honest aligned official-Qwen server benchmark this jumps the actual DFlash path from the corrected 37.38 tok/s baseline to 62.80 tok/s, clearing the 60 tok/s target.

Tune the new native context-only draft-cache path by shrinking `DFLASH_DRAFT_WINDOW` from 1024 to 256 on the corrected aligned official-Qwen server benchmark. A quick direct-runtime probe made 256 look promising, but the honest full server path dropped to 50.61 tok/s versus the kept 62.80 path, so discard; the direct sweep overpredicted and the current best remains the default window.

Tune the kept native context-only draft-cache path by shrinking the draft sink to zero (`DFLASH_DRAFT_SINK=0`) while keeping the default 1024 window on the corrected aligned official-Qwen server benchmark. This collapsed to 35.27 tok/s versus the kept 62.80 path, so discard; preserving some sink context is essential in the honest server path.

Retry the exact external bstnxbt draft-loader integration on the corrected greedy HTTP benchmark, since the old server regression was measured before the benchmark fix and was therefore stale. Add an env-gated `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_EXTERNAL_DRAFT=1` loader path in `omlx/dflash/drafter.py` that imports `external/dflash-mlx-bstnxbt`, uses its `load_draft_bundle(...)`, and overrides `make_cache()` to return the exact external `ContextOnlyDraftKVCache` shape. On the honest aligned official-Qwen server benchmark this reached 73.07 tok/s and then 72.11 tok/s on confirmation, a large real win over the 62.80 native context-only keep.

Test the external runtime’s own verify-length cap on top of the corrected external-draft keep by setting `DFLASH_VERIFY_LEN=8` while keeping the aligned official-Qwen server workload otherwise unchanged. This collapsed the honest server path to 29.09 tok/s versus the kept ~73 tok/s band, so discard; partial verify is not a safe optimization for this path.

Test whether the extra in-tree recurrent tape-kernel path is now just overhead on the corrected external-draft keep by disabling it (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_RECURRENT_KERNELS=0`) while keeping the aligned official-Qwen server workload otherwise unchanged. This collapsed the honest server path to 26.08 tok/s versus the kept ~72–73 tok/s band, so discard; recurrent rollback semantics remain essential even with the exact external draft integration.

Tune the target-side split-attention chunking on top of the corrected external-draft keep by setting `DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_CHUNK_SIZE=4` while keeping the aligned official-Qwen server workload otherwise unchanged. A quick direct runtime scout made chunk 4 look promising, but the honest full server path dropped to 47.55 tok/s versus the kept ~72–73 tok/s band, so discard; direct split-chunk sweeps are not predictive of server-path performance here.

Test whether the corrected external-draft keep still wants split full-attention SDPA by disabling it (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_SPLIT_SDPA=0`) while keeping the aligned official-Qwen server workload otherwise unchanged. This dropped the honest server path to 42.43 tok/s versus the kept ~72–73 tok/s band, so discard; split SDPA remains essential in the current high-throughput external-draft path.

Test the strongest remaining target-side parity idea by giving the oMLX DFlash runtime a separately loaded external bstnxbt target bundle (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_EXTERNAL_TARGET=1`) while keeping the corrected external draft integration and aligned official-Qwen server workload unchanged. This was catastrophically bad on the honest server path at 13.83 tok/s, so discard; the remaining gap is not solved by swapping in the full external target loader.

Re-run the current best corrected external-draft configuration unchanged to tighten the noise band before further code changes. Instead of reproducing the earlier 73.07 / 72.11 tok/s results, clean confirmation reruns came in at 38.06 and then 51.96 tok/s, both far below the original keep band. Discard this rerun pair as unstable/non-reproducible; the external-draft path is still promising, but its variance now needs explanation before treating 72–73 tok/s as steady-state.

name: Aligned official-Qwen DFlash server benchmark with warmed DFlash path

description: Make the benchmark warm the actual configured generation path instead of only stock one-token load warmup: parameterize `autoresearch.sh` with `WARMUP_DFLASH` and `WARMUP_MAX_TOKENS`, send fully greedy warmup params, and use a distinct warmup prompt so we warm DFlash compilation/runtime without warming the benchmark prefix. On the new warmed-DFlash-path benchmark segment, the corrected external-draft keep immediately reproduces the high band at 72.98 tok/s, showing the earlier 38/52 reruns were mostly cold-path benchmark noise rather than the steady-state ceiling.

Match the external benchmark more closely by loading the env-gated external draft bundle with `lazy=True` instead of `lazy=False` in `omlx/dflash/drafter.py`, while keeping the warmed-DFlash-path benchmark and the rest of the corrected external-draft keep unchanged. This collapsed the honest server path to 18.10 tok/s, so discard; eager external draft loading is currently essential in oMLX.

Port the external runtime’s `block_tokens` control into the live in-tree bstnxbt path via `DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS`, then test the strongest external direct hint on the warmed-DFlash-path benchmark: reduce effective speculative block size from the draft default 16 to 12 while keeping the exact external draft integration and all other warmed-path keep settings unchanged. This produced 83.53 tok/s and then 84.07 tok/s on immediate confirmation, lifting the honest oMLX server path above the aligned external ceiling.

Test the nearest plausible follow-up to the new `DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS=12` keep by lowering the external-runtime-inspired block override to 10 on the warmed-DFlash-path benchmark. Despite looking competitive in a direct external scout, the honest oMLX server path collapsed to 33.71 tok/s, so discard; the warmed server optimum is not following the direct sweep monotonically and 12 remains the current best.

name: Aligned official-Qwen DFlash server benchmark with block-matched DFlash warmup

description: Make the DFlash warmup benchmark contract follow the configured speculative block size by default: when `WARMUP_DFLASH=1` and no explicit warmup token count is set, `autoresearch.sh` now uses `DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS` (or 16 if unset) instead of a fixed token count. This avoids measuring cold block-size-specific DFlash compilation for tuned block schedules. On the fresh block-matched warmed-path segment, the current best external-draft + `DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS=12` configuration establishes an honest baseline at 84.52 tok/s.

name: OmniCoder main-workload transfer of warmed external-draft DFlash path

description: Start the Omni transfer segment by applying the current best warmed-DFlash-path learnings to the real OmniCoder 256-token main workload: warmed DFlash path enabled, exact external draft integration, split SDPA on, recurrent kernels on, and `DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS=12`. This establishes the first honest Omni transfer baseline at 27.09 tok/s.

Test the strongest Omni transfer candidate suggested by a quick direct scout: lower `DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS` from 12 to 8 while keeping the warmed external-draft path otherwise unchanged on the real OmniCoder 256-token workload. The honest server path regressed hard to 17.19 tok/s versus the 27.09 transfer baseline, so discard; direct in-process block sweeps are not predictive on Omni either.

name: Aligned official-Qwen DFlash server benchmark with thinking enabled

description: Re-target autoresearch to the honest warmed Qwen3.5 DFlash server benchmark with thinking enabled by wiring thinking controls through the harness and benchmarking the current bstnxbt external-draft path on a reasoning prompt.

asi.hypothesis: The first step toward 60-80 tok/s with thinking tokens is to benchmark the actual thinking-enabled DFlash server path honestly, using warmed DFlash requests and the same Qwen serving stack.
asi.learning: Current thinking-enabled warmed Qwen DFlash throughput is about 30.69 tok/s on a 1024-token reasoning workload. The main remaining gap is now in thinking-mode runtime/integration behavior rather than no-thinking benchmark plumbing.
asi.next_action_hint: Compare thinking-enabled request routing and prompt/rendered-shape behavior against the fast no-thinking path, then remove any avoidable thinking-mode fallback or shape penalties without cheating semantics.

name: Aligned official-Qwen DFlash server benchmark with thinking enabled (math prompt)

description: Correct the thinking-enabled Qwen benchmark workload to use the intended math reasoning prompt from workload.env and re-anchor the warmed DFlash server baseline on that real thinking workload.

asi.hypothesis: The thinking benchmark should be anchored on the intended reasoning prompt, not the fallback explanatory prompt, before tuning for 60-80 tok/s.
asi.learning: On the corrected math reasoning workload, the current warmed Qwen thinking-enabled DFlash server path runs at about 31.24 tok/s for 1024 tokens.
asi.next_action_hint: Attack the biggest likely lever next: tune DFlash block sizing and thinking-budget/request-shape behavior specifically for the thinking-enabled workload.

description: Retune the bstnxbt speculative block size for the thinking-enabled Qwen math workload by lowering DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS from 12 to 8.

asi.hypothesis: Thinking-enabled decoding likely has lower acceptance than the no-thinking path, so a smaller speculative block may reduce wasted draft/verify work and improve honest server throughput.
asi.learning: On the thinking-enabled warmed Qwen math workload, DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS=8 improved throughput from about 31.24 tok/s to 34.95 tok/s.
asi.next_action_hint: Continue the thinking-specific block-size sweep around 8 and then test other thinking-shape knobs such as thinking_budget once the block-size neighborhood is mapped.

description: Probe a smaller speculative block on the thinking-enabled Qwen math workload by lowering DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS from 8 to 6.

asi.hypothesis: If the thinking-enabled path still has low acceptance, shrinking the speculative block further from 8 to 6 might reduce wasted work and improve throughput.
asi.rollback_reason: DFLASH_BLOCK_TOKENS=6 regressed the honest thinking-enabled server benchmark from 34.95 tok/s to 30.43 tok/s.
asi.next_action_hint: Keep the new 8-token block baseline and continue sweeping nearby values upward instead of shrinking further.

description: Change the default warmup prompt for thinking-enabled runs to a short reasoning prompt instead of the generic warmup text.

description: Propagate `enable_thinking` through the general oMLX DFlash runtime so the bstnxbt path can apply the tuned 9-token thinking block-size default outside benchmark-only glue, and adjust the fast Qwen launcher so thinking-enabled warmup keeps the safer 16-token warmup length.

description: Productize the fast Qwen path further by fixing DFlash streaming detokenization, injecting the missing reasoning `<think>` prefix on the DFlash stream, and writing greedy-compatible sampling defaults into fast model settings so normal chat/API requests can stay on the server-side DFlash path without request-level sampling glue.

description: Taught scripts/siliconflash_start_qwen35_fast.sh to use the shared apply_dflash_fast_model_settings helper and added auth to launcher warmup/example requests. description: Minimal launcher-only productization patch: set the greedy sampling fields directly in scripts/siliconflash_start_qwen35_fast.sh model settings (temperature/top_p/top_k/min_p/force_sampling) and add auth to launcher warmup/example requests. asi.learning: The launcher/defaulting root cause is now isolated: compared with autoresearch’s helper, the launcher was missing the greedy sampling fields. Adding only those fields appears benchmark-neutral-ish (87.50 tok/s) rather than catastrophically bad, unlike the earlier helper-based launcher rewrite.

description: Slice committed_hidden to the accepted prefix immediately after verify, matching the external loop more closely and avoiding carrying rejected-step hidden states into the next cycle. It preserved correctness and stayed in-band, but did not beat the current best.

description: Add a no-tool server fast path that skips tool-call extraction work in non-stream chat/Anthropic/Responses requests when no tools were requested, and verify it with focused integration coverage. It cleaned the path but did not improve the authoritative broader-lane benchmark.

description: Probe the settings-driven thinking lane with explicit WARMUP_ENABLE_THINKING=1, since otherwise warmup inherits ENABLE_THINKING=0 even though model settings force thinking. It did not improve the broader benchmark path.

description: Add a server-side omitted-sampling fallback so DFlash-enabled models default to greedy-compatible sampling when the request omits sampling knobs. Despite targeted test coverage, this change regressed the authoritative broader lane badly and is not safe to keep.

name: Qwen long-context DFlash vs stock sweep (starting at 2046 tokens)

description: Honest 4000-token stock comparison under the same server settings as the 4000-token DFlash checkpoint, except with request-level DFlash disabled. On this context length, stock is currently faster than the tested DFlash shape.

asi.hypothesis: At 4000 completion tokens, DFlash may still outperform stock on the same real server workload under the current strongest recovery configuration.

description: Test the deferred long-context adaptive-block path honestly at 4000 completion tokens by enabling tail-only adaptive block sizing (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_ADAPTIVE_BLOCK=1`, start at 768`) on the explicit-request DFlash recovery shape. It improves DFlash at 4000, but still does not beat stock.

asi.hypothesis: The 1024-token lane showed neutral results for late adaptive block sizing, but at 4000 tokens the long tail may benefit enough for DFlash to catch or exceed stock.
asi.rollback_reason: Although adaptive block sizing improved DFlash at 4000 (~32.09 tok/s vs ~30.41), it still remained below the matched 4000-token stock checkpoint (~36.07 tok/s).

asi.learning: Tail-only adaptive block sizing is a real long-context lever: at 4000 tokens it improved DFlash from ~30.41 to ~32.09 tok/s, but DFlash still trails stock at this length.

description: Retest long-context adaptive block sizing at 4000 tokens with an earlier start (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_ADAPTIVE_BLOCK_START=256`) on the explicit-request DFlash recovery shape. This was worse than the tail-only 768-start version.

asi.next_action_hint: Keep any future adaptive-block experiments tail-only at long lengths; do not start shrink this early again.
asi.learning: At 4000 tokens, adaptive block sizing only helps when deferred into the tail; starting at 256 tokens is clearly harmful.

description: Test a nearby fixed block-size shift for long-context DFlash by moving from block 15 to block 14 at 4000 tokens on the explicit-request recovery shape with cache256. It regressed heavily.

description: Test the other nearby fixed block-size shift for long-context DFlash by moving from block 15 to block 16 at 4000 tokens on the explicit-request recovery shape with cache256. It remained worse than block 15.

description: Retest long-context adaptive block sizing at 4000 tokens with a later tail-only start (`DFLASH_BSTNXBT_ADAPTIVE_BLOCK_START=1024`) on the explicit-request DFlash recovery shape. It was worse than the 768-start version.

description: Test the broader normal-request DFlash lane at 4000 completion tokens with request-driven thinking and INITIAL_CACHE_BLOCKS=256, to see whether the normal-request shape beats the explicit-request DFlash variant at this longer decode length.
